description: RAG Database for English Grammar Restructuring - Natural Idiom Patterns for JP→EN Translation (Extended via 148-EPUB corpus analysis + Tofugu Grammar reinforcement: transitive/intransitive pairs, pronoun omission recovery, こそあど demonstratives, plural suffixes, verbal nouns, な-adjective rendering, date/time ordering). v2.3: Tier 2/3 Grammar expansion — 16 new categories: adjective_sa_mi_nominalizers, chuu_juu_naka_readings, dake_restriction_particle, nda_ndesu_explanatory, tagaru_third_person_desire, jibun_reflexive, ga_kedo_conjunctive, mada_mou_continuation, nara_conditional, noni_conjunction, particle_mo_additive, shi_reason_listing, nagara_simultaneous_contrast, tarisuru_incomplete_list, tearu_intentional_state, toki_time_clause.

contrastive_comparison.description: Comparing two subjects where both share a quality, but one is notable/surprising

patterns.japanese_structure: AはBだが、CもBだ / AはBけど、CはBだ
patterns.english_pattern: X is one thing, but Y is [quality]

examples.jp: 真理亜は変だが、如月さんも結構変だ
examples.literal: Maria's weird, but Kisaragi-san is pretty weird in her own right
examples.natural: Maria's one thing, but Kisaragi-san is pretty weird in her own right

usage_rules: both subjects share the same quality
First subject sets baseline expectation
Second subject exceeds/matches in surprising way

patterns.japanese_structure: だけでなく / ばかりでなく
patterns.english_pattern: not just X, but Y / not only X but (also) Y

examples.jp: 彼女は美しいだけでなく、頭もいい
examples.literal: She is not only beautiful, but also smart
examples.natural: She's not just beautiful—she's brilliant too

usage_rules: Use em dash (—) for emphasis in casual writing
'not just' is more conversational than 'not only'
Can drop 'also' for punchier delivery

patterns.japanese_structure: はおろか / どころか
patterns.english_pattern: let alone X / much less X / never mind X

examples.jp: 歩くどころか、立つこともできない
examples.literal: Forget about walking, I can't even stand
examples.natural: I can't even stand, let alone walk

usage_rules: 'let alone' is most natural in modern English
Order: lesser impossibility comes first
Emphasizes extreme inability

dismissive_acknowledgment.description: Acknowledging something while dismissing its importance

patterns.japanese_structure: Aはともかく / Aは置いといて
patterns.english_pattern: X aside, ... / Putting X aside, ...

examples.jp: 君はともかく、彼女は絶対怒る
examples.literal: Putting you aside, she would definitely get mad
examples.natural: You're one thing, but she'd definitely get mad

usage_rules: dismissing first subject to focus on second
Can combine with 'one thing' pattern

intensifiers.description: Japanese emotional/degree markers that need natural English restructuring (aligned with grammar_pattern_detector category)

negative_vectors.description: Suppress match when source implies certainty or understatement, not emotional intensity
texts: completely certain, absolutely, without a doubt, definitely, 100 percent sure
barely, hardly, a little bit, not very, slightly, faintly
factual statement, objective description, technical specification

patterns.japanese_structure: めちゃくちゃ / 超 / すごく + adjective
patterns.english_pattern: crazy [adjective] / insanely [adjective] / ridiculously [adjective]

examples.jp: めちゃくちゃエロい
examples.literal: extremely lewd
examples.natural: crazy lewd / insanely hot
examples.context: Casual speech, light novel protagonist internal monologue

usage_rules: Match register: めちゃくちゃ → crazy/insanely (casual)
超 → super/crazy (youth slang)
すごく → really/incredibly (neutral)

patterns.japanese_structure: 絶対 + verb/adjective (strong determination/prohibition)
patterns.english_pattern: Context-dependent natural emphasis (not 'absolutely')

examples.jp: 絶対に行く
examples.literal: I will absolutely go
examples.natural: I'm going for sure / I'm so going / No way I'm missing this

usage_rules: Avoid literal 'absolutely' unless formal/serious context
Prohibition: prefer 'no way', 'not happening', 'never'
Promise: use 'I swear', 'for real', 'cross my heart'

patterns.japanese_structure: まったく/全く (exasperation/complete negation/agreement)
patterns.english_pattern: Context-dependent: exasperation vs negation vs agreement

examples.jp: まったく、しょうがないな
examples.literal: Really, it can't be helped
examples.natural: Good grief / Honestly / Jeez, what am I gonna do with you

usage_rules: Exasperation: 'good grief', 'honestly', 'jeez' (gentle fr...
Complete negation: 'not at all', 'zero', 'haven't the fai...
Agreement: 'exactly', 'tell me about it', 'ain't that the...

high_frequency_transcreations.description: Most common Japanese expressions needing context-aware transcreation (based on 400K lines corpus analysis) Includes explicit contraction ICL for past-tense narrative and dialogue register alignment.

patterns.japanese_structure: Past-tense narrative introspection with contractable auxiliaries (〜ていた / 〜だった / 推量)
patterns.english_pattern: In past-tense narration, contract auxiliaries when natural: I'd / he'd / she'd / we'd / they'd, would've, must've, could've, should've

examples.jp: あのとき、私はそうするべきだったと思った。
examples.literal: At that time, I thought I should have done that.
examples.natural: Back then, I thought I should’ve done that.

examples.jp: きっと彼は気づいていたはずだ。
examples.literal: Surely he must have noticed.
examples.natural: Surely he must’ve noticed.

usage_rules: Prefer contractions in introspective first-person/close-third narration unless emphasis or formal register requires full forms.
High-confidence narration contractions: I'd been, I'd seen, would've, must've, couldn't, didn't.
Keep readability: do not stack excessive contractions in one short clause.

patterns.japanese_structure: Past-state description with copula (〜だった / 〜でいた)
patterns.english_pattern: Do NOT force contractions where none exist: I was / you were / he was / they were / we were remain full forms

examples.jp: そのとき私は不安だった。
examples.literal: At that time I was anxious.
examples.natural: At that time, I was anxious.
examples.context: Correct full form in past narrative

examples.jp: 彼らはその場にいた。
examples.literal: They were there.
examples.natural: They were there.

examples.jp: 俺たちはもうカップルではない。
examples.literal: We are no longer a couple.
examples.natural: We weren't a couple anymore.
examples.context: CRITICAL: 俺たち/私たち/二人 = plural 'we' — ALWAYS 'we were', NEVER 'we was'

examples.jp: ソファ席に案内された俺たちは、二人揃って注文した。
examples.literal: We, shown to a sofa seat, both ordered.
examples.natural: We were shown to a booth and both ordered the same thing.
examples.context: 俺たち + passive 案内された = 'we were shown' — plural, SVA must agree

usage_rules: Never invent non-forms like *I's or *they's or *we's for past be-verbs.
If a clause uses was/were as main copula, keep full form and contract surrounding auxiliaries instead.
Do not treat valid was/were as contraction failures during style optimization.
CRITICAL SVA: 俺たち/私たち/二人 (we/us/the two of us) = PLURAL — always 'we were', never 'we was'.
Zero-pronoun JP sentences with plural context still require 'we were' in EN, not 'we was'.

patterns.japanese_structure: JP plural first-person/pair pronouns with past copula (俺たち/私たち/二人 + だった/でいた/〜ていた)
patterns.english_pattern: JP plural pronouns MUST map to 'we were' (not 'we was') in past-tense EN
patterns.sva_error_to_avoid: we was / we wasn't / we weren't → always 'we were' / 'we weren't'

examples.jp: 俺たちは付き合っていたのは十代の時だった。
examples.literal: We was dating when we were teenagers.
examples.natural: We were dating back when we were still teenagers.
examples.context: 俺たち = explicit plural subject; 'was' is a critical SVA error here

examples.jp: 私たちはもう別れていて、お互いに今を楽しむ相手もいる。
examples.literal: We was already broken up, and we both had someone to enjoy the present with.
examples.natural: We were already broken up, and we both had someone new.
examples.context: 私たち = explicit plural (Hikari's POV); SVA must agree with plural

examples.jp: 強情な俺たちは、そこから一切引けなくなっていって。
examples.literal: Stubborn as we was, neither of us could back down.
examples.natural: Stubborn as we were, neither of us could back down from there.
examples.context: 俺たち adjective-noun construction is still a plural subject

examples.jp: 実際は凍えきった元カップルなんだけど。（ゼロ主語文）
examples.literal: In reality, we was a frozen-over ex-couple.
examples.natural: In reality, we were a frozen-over ex-couple.
examples.context: Zero-pronoun sentence — subject 'we' implied by narrative context; still plural, still 'were'

examples.jp: 最初は夕食だけの約束だったけれど。（約束＝singular subject）
examples.literal: We was only supposed to have dinner at first.
examples.natural: It was only supposed to be dinner at first.
examples.context: SPECIAL CASE: JP subject is 約束 (the plan/arrangement = singular 'it'), NOT 'we'. Misreading as 'we' is a double error.
examples.error_pattern: We was only supposed to → ❌ (wrong subject + wrong copula)
examples.correct_pattern: It was only supposed to be → ✅

usage_rules: 俺たち / 私たち / 二人 are ALWAYS plural subjects — unconditionally map to 'we were' in past EN.
No register exception: comedic, high-tension, flashback, casual — all require 'we were'.
Zero-pronoun sentences with plural narrative context still require 'we were'.
SUBJECT CHECK: if JP source has 約束/話/予定 (plan/arrangement/schedule) as subject, use 'it was' not 'we were'.
Grammar RAG rule narration_keep_uncontractable_was_were applies: 'we was' is an invented non-form, never valid.
patterns.rationale: Closes SVA regression gap identified in 17ed (マッチングアプリで元恋人と再会した。) — 10 'We was' errors traced to missing JP plural-pronoun ICL disambiguation in the grammar RAG.

patterns.japanese_structure: Casual spoken dialogue in present tense (会話体)
patterns.english_pattern: In casual dialogue, default to present-tense contractions: I'm, you're, it's, don't, can't, won't

examples.jp: 今は行けないよ。
examples.literal: I cannot go now.
examples.natural: I can’t go right now.

examples.jp: 私は大丈夫だよ。
examples.literal: I am fine.
examples.natural: I’m fine.

usage_rules: Dialogue-first rule: contract by default unless character profile requires formal speech.
Character exceptions are valid (e.g., ojou-sama/formal upbringing), but must be deliberate and consistent.
When uncertain, choose the spoken form a native teen would say aloud.

patterns.japanese_structure: Over-compressed English output from mechanical contraction pass
patterns.english_pattern: Prefer natural contraction boundaries: I'd have / he'd have (avoid stacked forms like I'd've in narrative prose)

examples.jp: そうしていたら、私は手伝っていただろう。
examples.literal: In that case, I would have helped.
examples.natural: In that case, I’d have helped.

examples.jp: 彼はすぐに気づいただろう。
examples.literal: He would have noticed quickly.
examples.natural: He’d have noticed quickly.

usage_rules: Use 'd + have as the safe default in narration polish.
Avoid stacked contractions when they reduce readability or look machine-generated.
Prioritize natural prose flow over maximizing contraction count.

patterns.japanese_structure: やっぱり / やはり / やっぱ (confirmed expectation/reversion)
patterns.english_pattern: Context-dependent: confirmation vs reversion vs opinion

examples.jp: やっぱり彼女は可愛い
examples.literal: As expected, she is cute
examples.natural: She really is cute / Yeah, she's definitely cute

usage_rules: Confirmed expectation: 'sure enough', 'definitely', 'just...
Reversion: 'actually', 'on second thought', 'never mind'
Opinion reinforcement: 'really', 'seriously', intensified...

patterns.japanese_structure: まあ / まぁ (hedging/downplaying/acceptance)
patterns.english_pattern: Context-dependent filler/hedge

examples.jp: まあ、いいけど
examples.literal: Well, it's fine but
examples.natural: I guess that's fine / Fair enough / Sure, whatever

usage_rules: Reluctant acceptance: 'I guess', 'sure, whatever', 'fine'
Partial agreement: 'pretty much', 'more or less'
Downplaying: 'not bad', 'decent', 'could be worse'

patterns.japanese_structure: さすが / 流石 (impressed expectation)
patterns.english_pattern: Impressed acknowledgment of expected excellence

examples.jp: さすが真理亜だ
examples.literal: As expected of Maria
examples.natural: That's Maria for you / Classic Maria / Leave it to Maria

usage_rules: Praise: 'That's [name] for you', 'Leave it to [name]', 'C...
Limit: 'Even [subject] can't', 'That's too much'
Admiration: 'Now that's a [role]', 'What a [role]'

patterns.japanese_structure: なんだか / 何だか (vague feeling)
patterns.english_pattern: Expressing vague/uncertain feeling

examples.jp: なんだか嬉しい
examples.literal: Somehow I'm happy
examples.natural: I'm kinda happy / There's something nice about this / I don't know why but I'm happy

usage_rules: Vague emotion: 'kinda', 'sort of', 'for some reason'
Suspicion: 'something's off', 'there's something [adj]'
Casual: 'I don't know why but'

patterns.japanese_structure: 別に (tsundere dismissal/actual indifference)
patterns.english_pattern: Dismissive or genuinely indifferent response

examples.jp: 別に気にしてないし
examples.literal: I'm not particularly caring
examples.natural: It's not like I care / Whatever / Doesn't bother me

usage_rules: Tsundere: 'It's not like I [verb]' (classic pattern)
Indifference: 'whatever', 'sure', 'fine by me'
Denial: 'That's not...' with trailing off

patterns.japanese_structure: 確かに / たしかに (concession/recollection)
patterns.english_pattern: Agreement/concession vs memory recall

examples.jp: 確かにそうだ
examples.literal: Certainly that's so
examples.natural: You've got a point / Fair point / Can't argue with that

usage_rules: Concession: 'Fair point', 'You're right', 'Good point'
Memory: 'If I remember', 'I think', 'wasn't she...?'
AVOID: 'Certainly' unless formal context

patterns.japanese_structure: まさか (disbelief/impossible scenario)
patterns.english_pattern: Expressing shock or disbelief

examples.jp: まさか、本当に来たの？
examples.literal: No way, did you really come?
examples.natural: Wait, you actually came? / Hold on, you're really here?

usage_rules: Surprise: 'Wait', 'Hold on', 'No way'
Denial: 'That can't be', 'There's no way', 'Impossible'
Rhetorical: 'Don't tell me...', 'Could it be...?'

patterns.japanese_structure: もしかして / ひょっとして (tentative guess)
patterns.english_pattern: Tentative question or realization

examples.jp: もしかして、怒ってる？
examples.literal: By any chance, are you angry?
examples.natural: Are you... mad at me? / Wait, are you upset?

usage_rules: Question: 'Wait, are you...?', 'Could you be...?'
Realization: 'Don't tell me...', 'Hold on, is this...?'
Soften with pause: 'Are you... [dramatic pause]'

patterns.japanese_structure: とりあえず (for now/anyway)
patterns.english_pattern: Provisional action or starting point

examples.jp: とりあえずビール
examples.literal: For the time being, beer
examples.natural: I'll start with a beer / Beer for now

usage_rules: Ordering: 'I'll start with', '[item] for now'
Action: '[verb] first', 'Let me just [verb]'
Tentative: 'for now', 'to start with'

patterns.japanese_structure: そもそも (in the first place/fundamentally)
patterns.english_pattern: Questioning premise or returning to basics

examples.jp: そもそも、なぜここに？
examples.literal: In the first place, why here?
examples.natural: Wait, why are you even here? / The real question is, why are you here?

usage_rules: Questioning: 'The real question is', 'Why even', 'Wait, why'
Original state: 'from the start', 'to begin with', 'never...
Fundamental: 'at its core', 'fundamentally'

patterns.japanese_structure: 仕方ない / しょうがない (can't be helped)
patterns.english_pattern: Resigned acceptance

examples.jp: 仕方ないな
examples.literal: It can't be helped
examples.natural: Well, what can you do / Guess there's no choice / Oh well

usage_rules: Resignation: 'Oh well', 'What can you do', 'That's life'
Reluctant help: 'Fine, I'll [verb]', 'Guess I have to'
Philosophical: 'These things happen', 'Such is life'

patterns.japanese_structure: せっかく (since you went to the trouble)
patterns.english_pattern: Acknowledging effort/opportunity

examples.jp: せっかく来たんだから
examples.literal: Since you went to the trouble of coming
examples.natural: Since you're already here / Now that you've come all this way

usage_rules: Opportunity: 'Since you're here', 'Now that [situation]'
Wasted effort: 'After all that effort', 'And I worked so ...
Encouragement: 'It'd be a shame not to', 'Might as well'

patterns.japanese_structure: わざわざ (going out of one's way)
patterns.english_pattern: Emphasizing deliberate effort

examples.jp: わざわざ来てくれたの？
examples.literal: Did you go out of your way to come?
examples.natural: You came all the way here? / You didn't have to come

usage_rules: Appreciation: 'all the way', 'You didn't have to'
Criticism: 'No need to', 'Why bother [verb]ing'
Sarcasm: 'Thanks for pointing that out' (ironic)

patterns.japanese_structure: 結局 (in the end/after all)
patterns.english_pattern: Concluding despite expectations

examples.jp: 結局、行かなかった
examples.literal: In the end, I didn't go
examples.natural: I ended up not going / I never did go

usage_rules: Outcome: 'ended up [verb]ing', 'in the end'
Realization: 'So that's', 'It all comes down to'
Summary: 'Bottom line', 'At the end of the day'

patterns.japanese_structure: 一応 (just in case/technically/sort of)
patterns.english_pattern: Hedging or minimal fulfillment

examples.jp: 一応聞いておく
examples.literal: I'll tentatively ask
examples.natural: I'll ask just in case / Let me check to be safe

usage_rules: Precaution: 'just in case', 'to be safe'
Qualification: 'technically', 'sort of', 'I guess'
Minimal: 'at least', 'for what it's worth'

patterns.japanese_structure: なるほど (understanding/agreement)
patterns.english_pattern: Acknowledging comprehension

examples.jp: なるほど、そういうことか
examples.literal: I see, so that's how it is
examples.natural: Ah, that makes sense / Oh, I get it now / So that's what's going on

usage_rules: Understanding: 'I see', 'Makes sense', 'I get it'
Realization: 'Ah', 'Oh', 'So that's why'
Casual: 'Gotcha', 'Right', 'Fair enough'

patterns.japanese_structure: ちょっと (softening/degree/attention)
patterns.english_pattern: Multi-purpose softener/intensifier

examples.jp: ちょっと待って
examples.literal: Wait a little
examples.natural: Hold on / Hang on / Wait up

usage_rules: Attention: 'Hey', 'Hold on', 'Wait up'
Degree: 'kind of', 'a bit', 'somewhat'
Softening: 'a little', or often just omit

patterns.japanese_structure: そういえば / ところで / それより (topic change)
patterns.english_pattern: Natural topic transitions

examples.jp: そういえば、明日は？
examples.literal: Speaking of which, tomorrow?
examples.natural: Oh, that reminds me—tomorrow? / Speaking of, what about tomorrow?

usage_rules: そういえば: 'That reminds me', 'Speaking of which'
ところで: 'Anyway', 'So', 'By the way'
それより: 'Forget that', 'Never mind that', 'More importantly'

patterns.japanese_structure: どうも (vague feeling/greeting/thanks)
patterns.english_pattern: Context-dependent filler

examples.jp: どうもおかしい
examples.literal: Somehow strange
examples.natural: Something's definitely off / I can't shake this feeling

usage_rules: Suspicion: 'Something's off', 'I can't shake [feeling]'
Greeting: 'Hey', 'Hi there', 'Yo'
Thanks: 'Thanks', 'Cheers'

patterns.japanese_structure: なんとか / どうにか (barely managing)
patterns.english_pattern: Expressing difficulty overcome

examples.jp: なんとか間に合った
examples.literal: Somehow made it in time
examples.natural: Just barely made it / Made it by the skin of my teeth

usage_rules: Success: 'just barely', 'managed to', 'pulled it off'
Optimism: 'It'll work out', 'We'll figure it out'
Request: 'Do something!', 'Figure it out!'

patterns.japanese_structure: ちなみに (additional info/by the way)
patterns.english_pattern: Casual information addition

examples.jp: ちなみに、俺は行かない
examples.literal: By the way, I'm not going
examples.natural: For the record, I'm not going / Just so you know, I'm out

usage_rules: Clarification: 'For the record', 'Just so you know'
Additional: 'By the way', 'Oh, and'
Can often move to end: '[statement], by the way'

logical_flow.description: Japanese logical connectors that need natural English equivalents

patterns.japanese_structure: だって / なぜなら at start of explanation
patterns.english_pattern: After all, ... / I mean, ... / It's just that ...

examples.jp: だって、真理亜は幼馴染だから
examples.literal: Because Maria is my childhood friend
examples.natural: After all, she's my childhood friend / I mean, Maria's my childhood friend

usage_rules: Use 'After all' for reasoning/justification
Use 'I mean' for clarification in casual speech
Don't start with bare 'Because' in English

emphasis_restructuring.description: Japanese emphasis particles that need English restructuring

patterns.japanese_structure: なんて / なんか with dismissive nuance
patterns.english_pattern: something like X / X or whatever / as if X

examples.jp: 彼女なんていらない
examples.literal: I don't need something like a girlfriend
examples.natural: I don't need a girlfriend or anything / Who needs a girlfriend?

usage_rules: なんて often implies dismissiveness
Can be sarcastic or genuinely dismissive
Context determines tone

double_negatives.description: Japanese double negatives that sound awkward in literal English

patterns.japanese_structure: ～ないわけにはいかない
japanese_indicators: ないわけにはいかない
patterns.english_pattern: have to [verb] / can't not [verb] / must [verb]

examples.jp: やらないわけにはいかない
examples.literal: It's not that I can't not do it
examples.natural: I have to do it / I can't just not do it

usage_rules: Prefer single positive statement when possible
Keep double negative only for emphatic effect
Match formality level

rhetorical_questions.description: Japanese rhetorical questions requiring English restructuring

patterns.japanese_structure: ～じゃない？ / ～だろ？ seeking agreement
patterns.english_pattern: right? / don't you think? / isn't it?

examples.jp: 変じゃない？
examples.literal: Isn't it weird?
examples.natural: That's weird, right? / Kinda weird, don't you think?

usage_rules: Place 'right?' at end for casual speech
Use 'don't you think?' for softer tone
Can restructure as statement + tag question

conditional_restructuring.description: Japanese conditional forms requiring natural English equivalents

patterns.japanese_structure: さえ～ば / ～さえすれば
patterns.english_pattern: if only X / as long as X / all you need is X

examples.jp: 君さえいれば、他に何もいらない
examples.literal: If only you are here, I don't need anything else
examples.natural: As long as I have you, I don't need anything else

usage_rules: 'if only' often implies wistful impossibility
'as long as' is more confident/assertive
'all you need' works for positive emphasis

patterns.japanese_structure: ～たら～ただろう (past counterfactual)
patterns.english_pattern: if X had [past], Y would have [past participle]

examples.jp: もっと早く来ていたら、彼女に会えただろう
examples.literal: If I came earlier, I could have met her
examples.natural: If I'd come earlier, I would've met her / Had I come earlier, I'd have met her

usage_rules: Use contracted forms for casual speech (I'd, would've)
Formal: can invert to 'Had I come...'
Past counterfactual requires 'would have' not 'would'

patterns.japanese_structure: ～限り / ～以上
patterns.english_pattern: provided (that) / as long as / so long as

examples.jp: 約束を守る限り、手伝ってあげる
examples.literal: As long as you keep your promise, I'll help you
examples.natural: I'll help you, provided you keep your promise

usage_rules: 'provided' is more formal/contractual
'as long as' is casual and common
Can place condition before or after main clause

perfect_aspect_nuances.description: Subtleties in perfect tense usage for completed actions with present relevance

patterns.japanese_structure: ～たことがある (experience)
patterns.english_pattern: have [past participle] / have been to

examples.jp: 日本に行ったことがある
examples.literal: I went to Japan before
examples.natural: I've been to Japan / I've visited Japan

usage_rules: Use present perfect for indefinite past experience
Simple past only if specific time mentioned
'have been to' specifically for places visited

patterns.japanese_structure: ～ている (ongoing from past)
patterns.english_pattern: have been [verb+ing]

examples.jp: ずっと待っているんだ
examples.literal: I am waiting for a long time
examples.natural: I've been waiting forever / I've been waiting this whole time

usage_rules: Use for actions starting in past, continuing now
Emphasizes duration with 'forever', 'all day', etc.
More emphatic than simple present

patterns.japanese_structure: すでに～ていた (prior completion)
patterns.english_pattern: had already [past participle] / had [past participle] before

examples.jp: 彼が来る前に、私はすでに帰っていた
examples.literal: Before he came, I already went home
examples.natural: I'd already left by the time he arrived / I had left before he came

usage_rules: Use past perfect for earlier of two past events
'by the time' emphasizes temporal relationship
Can use 'already' for completed state

modal_verb_subtleties.description: Nuanced modal verb usage beyond basic can/will/should

patterns.japanese_structure: ～てもいいかも / ～た方がいいかも
patterns.english_pattern: might as well / may as well

examples.note: 彼女の → 'her' — pronoun possessive, NOT 'her's'

examples.jp: 八本の目が私の方を向いた
examples.note: Numeral + noun opener — no possessive; 'Eight' is a numeral, not a name

name_vs_pronoun_decision_tree: Is the word before the noun a PROPER NAME (person's name, honorific name)? → ADD 's
Is the word before the noun a POSSESSIVE PRONOUN (my/her/his/their/our/its/your)? → NOTHING to add, already correct
Is the word before the noun an ADJECTIVE or NUMERAL (slender/rigid/eight/pale)? → NOTHING to add, no possessive
Is the word before the noun a COMMON NOUN used as modifier? → usually no 's needed, evaluate case-by-case
usage_rules: NEVER flag possessive pronouns (my/her/his/their/our/its) as missing apostrophe-s
NEVER add 's after an adjective even if capitalized at sentence start
Possessive 's is ONLY for proper nouns (names) and indefinite pronouns (one's/someone's)
Validator false-positive class: capitalized non-name token at sentence start + body/expression noun — NOT a possessive error
patterns.gemini_note: Top JP→EN false-positive source. 彼女の = 'her', not 'her's'. 細い describes fingers, not a person named Slender. Apply the decision tree before any possessive correction.

transitive_intransitive_rendering.description: Rendering transitive vs intransitive Japanese verb pairs accurately. JP uses paired verbs where EN often uses the same verb — the presence or absence of a volitional agent changes translation strategy. (Source: Tofugu Grammar — Transitive and Intransitive Verbs)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Subject が] 自動詞 — intransitive: no を-object, action happens by itself
patterns.english_pattern: [Subject] [verb]ed — NO agent phrase; let it happen on its own

examples.jp: ドアが開いた。
examples.literal: The door opened (by itself).
examples.natural: The door opened.
examples.note: No agent — do NOT add 'someone' or passive 'was opened'

examples.jp: 電気が消えた。
examples.literal: The lights went out.
examples.natural: The lights went out.
examples.note: Intransitive 消える — not 'The lights were turned off'

examples.jp: 授業が始まる前に。
examples.literal: Before class begins.
examples.natural: Before class starts.
examples.note: 始まる is intransitive — class starts itself; 始める would imply someone starting it

usage_rules: CRITICAL: intransitive = no agent in English; do NOT add passive construction
EN 'The door opened' ≠ 'The door was opened' — the latter implies someone opened it
Check particle: を = transitive (agent present); が only = intransitive (no agent)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Agent が/は] [Object を] 他動詞 — transitive: energy transfers from agent to object
patterns.english_pattern: [Agent] [verb]ed [object] — active construction; agent must appear

examples.jp: ジェニーがドアを開けた。
examples.literal: Jenny opened the door.
examples.natural: Jenny opened the door.
examples.note: Transitive 開ける — agent Jenny is the doer

examples.jp: キャメロンが電気をつけた。
examples.literal: Cameron turned on the lights.
examples.natural: Cameron turned on the lights.
examples.note: Transitive つける — agent must be named or pronoun-recovered from context

usage_rules: If agent is dropped by pronoun omission, recover it from context (see pronoun_omission_recovery)
Transitive verb with omitted agent in JP → active EN with recovered pronoun, NOT passive
WRONG: 'The lights were turned on' when an agent is clearly present in context

patterns.japanese_structure: [Subject は/が] [Agent に] ～られた / ～された — adversative passive (victim perspective)
patterns.english_pattern: got [verb]ed / had [noun] [verb]ed — colloquial adversative; preserve victim nuance

examples.jp: 財布を盗まれた。
examples.literal: My wallet was stolen (adversative — I am the victim).
examples.natural: I got my wallet stolen. / My wallet got stolen.
examples.note: Adversative passive = negative outcome for subject; 'got' preserves the victim feel

examples.jp: 雨に降られた。
examples.literal: I was rained on (adversarial — caught in rain).
examples.natural: I got caught in the rain.
examples.note: Natural English idiom for adversative passive with weather

usage_rules: Adversative passive = victim suffered due to the action — use 'got' passive in casual register
Formal register: 'was [verb]ed' acceptable if tone matches
Do NOT use neutral passive ('The wallet was stolen') when victim perspective is the narrative focus

pronoun_omission_recovery.description: Japanese freely omits subjects and objects when clear from context. English requires explicit pronouns. This category governs register-correct pronoun recovery and first-person pronoun register signals. (Source: Tofugu Grammar — Personal Pronouns)

patterns.japanese_structure: Predicate-only sentence where subject is implicit from context
japanese_indicators: です/ます without explicit subject
patterns.english_pattern: Supply [I/she/he/they/we/it] from immediate context — match established POV

examples.jp: 食べる！
examples.literal: (I'll eat / She'll eat — context determines)
examples.natural: I will! / I'll eat! (if speaker responds)
examples.note: EN requires 'I' even where Japanese omits entirely

examples.jp: ジェニファーです。（自己紹介）
examples.literal: (I am) Jennifer.
examples.natural: I'm Jennifer.
examples.note: First encounter introduction: implicit subject = speaker = 'I'

usage_rules: Determine POV from paragraph/scene context before assigning pronoun
In narration: omitted subject = POV character → use established pronoun (he/she/I)
In dialogue: omitted subject typically = speaker → use 'I' unless context indicates second/third person
NEVER leave a subject gap in EN — always recover explicitly

patterns.japanese_structure: Transitive verb sentence where を-object is omitted
patterns.english_pattern: Supply [it/them/him/her/that/this] from immediate co-text

examples.jp: 食べた。（先行詞：ケーキ）
examples.literal: Ate it. → '[She] ate it.' once cake is established
examples.natural: She ate it.
examples.note: Object 'it' must appear in EN even if omitted in JP

usage_rules: Verify the antecedent from the preceding sentence or paragraph
Use 'it' for objects already in focus; 'them' for plural; avoid repeating full nouns more than once per paragraph
For human objects: use gendered pronoun from established character data

patterns.japanese_structure: First-person pronoun selection encodes speaker gender, age, and register
patterns.english_pattern: All → 'I' in EN — but encode register difference in VOICE, DICTION, SENTENCE LENGTH

examples.jp: 私は行きます。
examples.literal: I will go. (neutral/formal — adult female or polite context)
examples.natural: I'll go.

examples.jp: 僕が行くよ。
examples.literal: I'll go. (softer, younger-male or boyish nuance)
examples.natural: I'll go. / I'll handle it.
examples.register: softer diction, shorter sentences, less aggressive phrasing

examples.jp: 俺が行く。
examples.literal: I'll go. (rough, casual male — bravado or nonchalance)
examples.natural: I'll go. / I'll take care of it.
examples.register: blunt, clipped, confident phrasing; may use mild profanity in rough scenes

examples.jp: あたしが行く。
examples.literal: I'll go. (casual feminine — warm or tomboyish)
examples.natural: I'll go.
examples.register: casual, warm tone; softer vocabulary choices

examples.jp: わし／儂 が行く。
examples.literal: I'll go. (elderly male — archaic/rustic register)
examples.natural: I'll go. / I shall go.
examples.register: archaic or stiff phrasing; can use 'shall' or old-fashioned constructions

usage_rules: EN has only 'I' — carry register difference through vocabulary and syntax, NOT pronoun form
俺 speakers: shorter sentences, blunter phrasing, confident or aggressive tone
僕 speakers: politer phrasing, more hedging language, softer emotional expression
わし speakers: archaic diction, 'shall/shall not', slower sentence rhythm
Record each character's pronoun in series bible / character sheet and apply consistently

patterns.japanese_structure: Second-person pronoun selection — あなた, きみ, お前, てめえ, 貴様, そちら, etc.
patterns.english_pattern: All → 'you' in EN — encode familiarity/hostility in sentence register and adjacent expletives

examples.jp: あなたはどう思いますか？
examples.natural: What do you think? (neutral/polite)

examples.jp: お前、何してる？
examples.natural: Hey, what are you doing? / What the hell are you doing?
examples.register: Casual-rough; 'Hey' opener or mild expletive marks informality

examples.jp: 貴様！
examples.natural: You bastard! / You—!
examples.register: Hostile/contemptuous — expletive or truncated sentence for drama

usage_rules: お前/てめえ/貴様 → mark with aggression indicator in surrounding narration or interjection
きみ → softer/intimate 'you'; may not need special marking in casual romance scenes
そちら/あちら → 'you' in formal/polite context — keep formal diction

kosoado_demonstratives.description: こそあど言葉 (ko-so-a-do words) encode spatial and conceptual distance. Accurate this/that/there rendering depends on speaker-listener-object geometry. (Source: Tofugu Grammar — Ko-So-A-Do Words)

patterns.japanese_structure: こ- prefix: この/これ/ここ/こちら/こっち/こんな/こう — proximate to speaker
patterns.english_pattern: this / here / this kind of / like this — speaker-side proximity

examples.jp: この貝、かわいい！
examples.natural: This shell is so cute!
examples.note: 物が話者の近くにある — 'this'

examples.jp: こんな状況で冷静でいられない。
examples.natural: I can't stay calm in a situation like this.
examples.note: こんな = 'like this / this kind of'

examples.jp: こうすればいい。
examples.natural: Just do it like this. / This is how you do it.
examples.note: こう = 'like this / in this way'

usage_rules: Use 'this' when object/concept is near speaker or is what the speaker is focusing on
こんな → 'this kind of' / 'like this' — not just 'such'
こう → 'like this / in this way' — manner adverb

patterns.japanese_structure: そ- prefix: その/それ/そこ/そちら/そっち/そんな/そう — proximate to listener or mid-distance
patterns.english_pattern: that / there / that kind of / like that — listener-side or conversational reference

examples.jp: その貝もいいね！
examples.natural: That shell looks good too!
examples.note: Object in listener's hand — 'that'

examples.jp: そんなことないよ。
examples.natural: That's not true. / It's not like that.
examples.note: そんな = 'that kind of / like that'

examples.jp: そうだね。
examples.natural: Yeah, you're right. / That's true.
examples.note: そう here = agreement/acknowledgment — see sou_response pattern

usage_rules: その/それ/そこ → 'that/there' when object near listener or referenced in dialogue
そんな → 'that kind of' / 'like that' in dismissals or reactions
When speaker and listener are side-by-side, そ- can cover mid-distance (not close enough for こ-, not far enough for あ-)

patterns.japanese_structure: あ- prefix: あの/あれ/あそこ/あちら/あっち/あんな/ああ — distant from both speaker and listener; OR shared prior knowledge
patterns.english_pattern: that...over there / that [shared-knowledge referent] — distance or mutual memory

examples.jp: あれ、なんだろう！
examples.natural: What's that over there?
examples.note: Object far from both — 'that over there' or just 'that' with context

examples.jp: あの人、知ってる？
examples.natural: Do you know that person? / You know that guy?
examples.note: あの = shared/contextual reference — both speaker and listener have basis for identification

examples.jp: あんな性格じゃ友達できないよ。
examples.natural: With a personality like that, she'll never make friends.
examples.note: あんな = 'that kind of' — referencing something both parties have knowledge of

usage_rules: あ- words for shared knowledge (both speaker and listener know the referent) even when not physically distant
'that person' / 'that one over there': use 'that' in EN; add 'over there' only when physical distance is the point
あんな → 'that kind of / like that' with negative/critical nuance common in light novels

patterns.japanese_structure: ど- prefix: どの/どれ/どこ/どちら/どっち/どんな/どう/どいつ — question words
patterns.english_pattern: which / which one / where / which way / what kind of / how

examples.jp: どれ？
examples.natural: Which one? / Which?
examples.note: どれ = which one (from a set)

examples.jp: どっちが好き？
examples.natural: Which do you like better? / Which one?
examples.note: どっち = which of the two (binary choice)

examples.jp: どんな人？
examples.natural: What kind of person? / What's she like?
examples.note: どんな = 'what kind of' — open descriptive question

usage_rules: どれ (from 3+) → 'which one'; どっち (binary) → 'which' or 'which one'
どちら is polite form of どっち — use formal 'which' in polite dialogue
どう → 'how / what do you think' — often used for feedback/reaction solicitation

plural_suffix_rendering.description: Japanese plural suffixes (〜たち, 〜ら, 〜方, 〜ども) encode group identity and social register. EN doesn't have equivalent suffixes — carry meaning through word choice and context. (Source: Tofugu Grammar — Plural and Quantity)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Noun/Name] + たち — person plus associated group; most common and neutral plural suffix
patterns.english_pattern: [name] and [their group/crew/friends] / we / they — inclusive grouping

examples.jp: 私たちは行きます。
examples.natural: We're going.
examples.note: 私たち = 'we' — standard first-person plural

examples.jp: ジェニー達は楽しそうだった。
examples.natural: Jenny and her group seemed to be having fun. / Jenny and the others looked like they were having fun.
examples.note: [Name]たち = named character + associates; not just Jenny alone

examples.jp: 子供たちが走ってきた。
examples.natural: The children came running.
examples.note: Generic plural — 'children' suffices; 'たち' adds no further nuance here

usage_rules: [Name]たち ≠ 'Jennys' — means Jenny and whoever she is with
For pronouns: 私たち → 'we', あなたたち → 'you all/you guys'
For generic nouns (子供たち, 友達たち): simple EN plural usually suffices

patterns.japanese_structure: [Noun/Pronoun] + ら — casual, rough, or slightly dismissive plural; common in male speech
patterns.english_pattern: we / you guys / those guys / them — casual-to-rough register

examples.jp: 俺らで何とかしよう。
examples.natural: Let's handle it ourselves. / We'll figure it out.
examples.note: 俺ら = rough 'we' — maintain bravado register

examples.jp: あいつらのことは気にするな。
examples.natural: Don't worry about those guys. / Forget about them.
examples.note: あいつら = dismissive 'they' / 'those guys'

examples.jp: お前ら、聞いてるか？
examples.natural: Hey, you guys listening? / Are you lot paying attention?
examples.note: お前ら = rough 'you guys / you lot'

usage_rules: 〜ら carries dismissive or casual-masculine flavor — use 'guys', 'them', or rough 'you lot'
Do NOT use 〜ら register for polite characters — check character voice
やつら = 'those guys / that bunch' — always has some dismissive edge

patterns.japanese_structure: [Noun] + 方(がた) — respectful plural; formal address of a group
patterns.english_pattern: you all / all of you / ladies and gentlemen / the [title] — formal plural

examples.jp: 先生方にお礼申し上げます。
examples.natural: I would like to express my gratitude to all the teachers.
examples.note: 先生方 = respectful 'the teachers / all the teachers'

examples.jp: 皆様方、本日はお越しいただき…
examples.natural: Ladies and gentlemen, thank you all for coming today…
examples.note: Formal address — 'ladies and gentlemen' or 'honored guests'

usage_rules: 〜方 = formal/respectful — translate with formal address or honorific phrasing
Never render 〜方 as casual 'guys' or 'you all' in serious/formal dialogue
In formal ceremony or official speech: 'ladies and gentlemen / honored guests / esteemed colleagues'

patterns.japanese_structure: [Pronoun] + ども — humble first-person plural OR contemptuous other-person plural
patterns.english_pattern: we (humble) / those fools / you lot (contemptuous) — context-dependent

examples.jp: 私どもにお任せください。
examples.natural: Please leave it to us. (business/formal humble)
examples.note: 私ども = humble 'we' in formal business register

examples.jp: こいつどもめ！
examples.natural: You fools! / You lot!
examples.note: Contemptuous — 'those fools', 'you idiots', 'this rabble'

usage_rules: 私ども = humble formal 'we/us' — company reps, servants, formal apology contexts
Contemptuous ども → expletive plural: 'you fools / you lot / this rabble'
Never use 私ども in casual narration — it is strictly formal/keigo register

verbal_noun_preference.description: Japanese verbal nouns (する verbs, nominalizations with こと/の) should be rendered as verbs in EN narration where possible. の functions as a placeholder noun (事/物/time/place/reason) whose implied meaning must be inferred from context and rendered explicitly in EN. (Source: Tofugu Grammar — Nouns + の Nominalizer)
verbal_noun_preference.tofugu_source: Nouns — Verbal Nouns (する Verb); Nominalizers; Particle の (Nominalizer)

patterns.japanese_structure: ～する / ～します — verbal noun + する
patterns.english_pattern: Prefer active verb over noun phrase in narration; noun phrase acceptable in formal/dialogue register

examples.jp: 説明します。
examples.literal: I will give an explanation.
examples.natural: I'll explain. / Let me explain.
examples.note: 説明する → 'explain', not 'give/offer an explanation'

examples.jp: 確認してください。
examples.natural: Please check. / Please confirm.
examples.note: 確認する → 'check/confirm', not 'perform a verification'

examples.jp: 感謝します。
examples.natural: Thank you. / I'm grateful.
examples.note: 感謝する → 'thank / be grateful', not 'express gratitude'

usage_rules: PREFER: verb form in narration (lighter, more natural)
ALLOW: noun phrase in formal speech, official announcements, or when the noun is the focus
WARNING: 'Do a [noun]' constructions often read as AI-generated — prefer single transitive verb
AI-ISM BLOCKLIST: 'give an explanation', 'make an apology', 'perform an inspection' → explain/apologize/inspect

patterns.japanese_structure: ～こと / ～の — nominalizer turning verb clause into noun clause
patterns.english_pattern: gerund ('-ing') or infinitive ('to [verb]') — match EN clause role

examples.jp: 泳ぐことが好きだ。
examples.literal: Swimming is something I like.
examples.natural: I love swimming. / Swimming is my thing.
examples.note: ～ことが好き → gerund as subject: 'Swimming [is fun]'

examples.jp: 早く寝ることを勧める。
examples.literal: I recommend going to bed early.
examples.natural: I'd recommend getting to bed early.
examples.note: ～ことを勧める → 'recommend [gerund]'

examples.jp: 走るのは体にいい。
examples.literal: Running is good for the body.
examples.natural: Running is good for you.
examples.note: ～のは (topic nominalizer) → gerund as sentence topic

usage_rules: こと/の as subject → EN gerund ('-ing') as grammatical subject
こと/の as object of 'begin/start/stop/like/hate' → EN gerund
こと as object of 'decide/want/plan/recommend' → EN infinitive 'to [verb]'
Do NOT translate ～こと as 'the matter of' or 'the act of' in casual narration — too formal

CRITICAL: の is a context-dependent placeholder — its implied noun (person/time/place/reason) must be inferred and made explicit in EN. See no_contextual_placeholder and no_cleft_focus_sentence patterns.

patterns.japanese_structure: [clause] + の — の acts as placeholder whose implied meaning depends on sentence context and what follows
patterns.tofugu_source: Particle の (Nominalizer) — Uses of Nominalizer の
patterns.english_pattern: Infer implied noun from context: PERSON → 'the one who'; TIME → 'when / it is [time] that'; PLACE → 'where / it is [place] that'; REASON → 'why / the reason'

implied_noun_inference_table.context_clue: Followed by person name or 誰
Followed by time expression (明日/今夜/etc.)
Followed by place name (東京/ここ/etc.)
Followed by reason (～から/～ため/などで)

examples.jp: 走るのはキャメロンだよ。
examples.literal: The one running is Cameron.
examples.natural: Cameron's the one who's running. / It's Cameron who's running.

examples.jp: スーツケースが届くのは明日です。
examples.literal: The time the suitcase will be delivered is tomorrow.
examples.natural: The suitcase arrives tomorrow. / It's tomorrow that the suitcase gets delivered.

examples.jp: 生まれたのはニューヨークです。
examples.literal: Where I was born is New York.
examples.natural: I was born in New York. / New York is where I was born.

examples.jp: アメリカに住みたいのは、英語の勉強がしたいからです。
examples.literal: The reason I want to live in the U.S. is because I want to study English.
examples.natural: I want to live in the U.S. because I want to study English. / The reason I want to live there is to study English.

examples.jp: 甘いのが食べたい。
examples.literal: I want to eat sweet things.
examples.natural: I want something sweet. / I'm craving something sweet.

examples.jp: 昔飼っていたのは、犬です。
examples.literal: What I used to have as a pet was a dog.
examples.natural: I used to have a dog. / The pet I had back then was a dog.

examples.jp: そこにいるのは、誰ですか？
examples.literal: Who is the person that is there?
examples.natural: Who's there?

usage_rules: STEP 1: Identify what follows [clause]の — a person? a time? a place? a reason?
STEP 2: Choose EN rendering from implied_noun_inference_table above
STEP 3: In narration, flatten cleft into natural EN if the cleft is purely structural (e.g. '生まれたのはNYです' → 'I was born in NY')
STEP 4: Preserve cleft ('It is X that...') ONLY when the context demands contrastive emphasis
NEVER translate contextual の literally as 'the thing that' in all cases — infer the actual implied noun

patterns.japanese_structure: [clause] + のは + [focus element] + です — cleft sentence placing emphasis on focus element
patterns.tofugu_source: Particle の (Nominalizer) — Time and Place + Relative Clause + の
patterns.english_pattern: Cleft: 'It is [focus] that [clause]' — OR flatten to natural EN when emphasis is structural rather than contrastive

cleft_vs_flatten_guide.use_cleft: When the focus element is being CORRECTED or CONTRASTED against an expectation
cleft_vs_flatten_guide.example: 届くのは明日じゃなくて今日です → 'It arrives TODAY, not tomorrow.'

cleft_vs_flatten_guide.use_cleft: When the focus is a surprise reveal or narrative punch
cleft_vs_flatten_guide.example: 走るのはお前だ → 'It's YOU who has to run.'

cleft_vs_flatten_guide.flatten: When the sentence is purely informational with no contrast
cleft_vs_flatten_guide.example: 生まれたのはNYです → 'I was born in New York.' (not 'It is New York that I was born in')

cleft_vs_flatten_guide.flatten: When the surrounding narration is casual/fast-paced — cleft slows rhythm unnecessarily

examples.jp: この町が美しかったのは、五十年前です。
examples.literal: When this city was beautiful is fifty years ago.
examples.natural_cleft: It was fifty years ago that this city was beautiful.
examples.natural_flat: This city was beautiful fifty years ago.
examples.preferred: flat — unless nostalgic contrast is the narrative point

examples.jp: 次のオリンピックが開催されるのは東京です。
examples.literal: The next Olympics will be held in Tokyo.
examples.natural_cleft: It's Tokyo that will host the next Olympics.
examples.natural_flat: Tokyo will host the next Olympics.

examples.jp: 大切なのはやさしさだ。
examples.literal: What's important is kindness.
examples.natural: What matters is kindness. / Kindness is what counts.
examples.note: な-adj + なのは → 'what is [adj] is X' — requires な before の

patterns.na_adj_rule: な-adjective + なのは (NOT ～のは): 大切なのは / 必要なのは / 嫌なのは — the な is REQUIRED before の in this construction
usage_rules: Default: flatten to natural EN for informational sentences
Use cleft ('It is X that...') for emphasis, correction, or surprise revelation
な-adjective version requires な before の — 大切なのは / 嫌なのは — do NOT drop な
Plain form required in the relative clause before の — NOT polite ～ます form

patterns.japanese_structure: Sentence + の at end — explanatory/assertive nuance; or の？ as curious question
patterns.tofugu_source: Particle の (Nominalizer) — の At The End of Sentences
patterns.english_pattern: Declarative の → assertive statement with explanatory undertone; Question の？ → curious or seeking-explanation question
patterns.register_note: Sentence-final の is informal; associated with feminine/childlike speech in declaratives, but gender-neutral in questions. Disappears in formal prose.

examples.jp: 授業中は、おしゃべりしないの！
examples.literal: [The rule is] No chatting during class!
examples.natural: No talking during class! / You don't talk during class!
examples.tone: assertive command with implied explanation — firm but not aggressive

examples.jp: まずは、洗うの。それで、きれいになったら、こうやって切るの。
examples.literal: First, clean it. Then, once clean, cut it like this.
examples.natural: First you wash it. Then once it's clean, you cut it like this.
examples.tone: instructional — step-by-step explanation; の reads as 'you [do X]' listing tone

examples.jp: まずは、洗うの？
examples.literal: Do you [normally] wash it first?
examples.natural: So you wash it first? / You clean it first?
examples.tone: genuine curiosity — rising intonation, no gender nuance

examples.jp: そういうのは、前もって教えてください。
examples.literal: Please let me know things like that in advance.
examples.natural: Give me a heads-up about things like that next time.
examples.note: そういうの = こそあど + の = 'things like that' — see kosoado_demonstratives

usage_rules: Declarative sentence-final の → render as plain assertion; add firmness through word choice ('You don't talk during class' not 'Please don't talk')
Instructional の listing → 'you [do X]' or imperative form; reads as step-by-step
Question の？ → 'So you [do X]?' / '[Do] you [do X]?' — curious, seeking confirmation; NOT demanding
Distinguish from んだ/のだ (stronger explanatory) — の alone is softer, more conversational
In male character speech: の at sentence end is unusual — flag as female/child speech marker if context is male character

patterns.japanese_structure: ～の、～のって / ～のなんのって — same adjective repeated positive + negative for extreme emphasis
patterns.tofugu_source: Particle の (Nominalizer) — Nominalizer の For Listing
japanese_indicators: すごいのすごくないの
美味しいの美味しくないの
patterns.english_pattern: 'beyond [adjective]' / 'incredibly [adjective] — you have no idea' / 'so [adjective] it's unreal'

examples.jp: ポートランドのビール、そりゃ美味しいの、美味しくないのって！
examples.literal: Beer in Portland, it's beyond a matter of delicious or not!
examples.natural: Portland beer is beyond good — it's on another level. / That beer? Absolutely incredible.
examples.note: Idiomatic hyperbole — 'whether it's good or not doesn't even apply anymore'

examples.jp: 泣くのなんのって、もう大変だったよ。
examples.literal: The crying was something else entirely.
examples.natural: Oh the crying — you should've seen it. It was insane. / We're talking serious waterworks.
examples.note: のなんのって = intensifier for uncontrollable/extreme degree

usage_rules: のなんのって / ～の～のって signals EXTREME degree — translate with hyperbolic EN phrasing
Avoid literal translation ('whether delicious or not') — it misses the intensifier function entirely
Register: casual, spoken — use colloquial EN ('insane', 'beyond', 'on another level', 'you have NO idea')
Common in light novel interior monologue and casual narration when a character encounters something overwhelming

patterns.japanese_structure: Abstract な-adjective used as grammatical noun (安全, 自由, 平和, 便利, 幸福)
patterns.english_pattern: Article + abstract noun — always needs 'the/a' in EN; no article in JP

examples.jp: 安全が第一だ。
examples.natural: Safety comes first. / Safety is the top priority.
examples.note: 安全 as noun → 'safety' (no article needed for abstract universal)

examples.jp: 自由を守りましょう。
examples.natural: Let's protect our freedom.
examples.note: 自由 as noun → 'freedom' — possessive 'our' added for naturalness

usage_rules: Abstract Japanese nouns often become mass nouns in EN — no article for universals (safety, peace, freedom)
When specific instance: add 'the' (e.g., 今の安全 → 'the current safety')
These words double as な-adjectives — role in sentence determines EN translation strategy

na_adjective_rendering.description: な-adjectives function as predicates, noun modifiers, adverbs (with に), and even nouns. EN translation strategy changes with grammatical role. (Source: Tofugu Grammar — な-Adjectives)

patterns.japanese_structure: な-adjective + に → adverb modifying a verb
patterns.english_pattern: adverb '-ly' form OR adverbial phrase — modifies the verb

examples.jp: 私は静かにドアを閉めた。
examples.natural: I quietly closed the door. / I closed the door without a sound.
examples.note: 静かに → 'quietly' — standard -ly adverb

examples.jp: バラが綺麗に咲いていた。
examples.natural: The roses were blooming beautifully.
examples.note: 綺麗に → 'beautifully / in full bloom' depending on visual emphasis

examples.jp: 急に泣き出した。
examples.natural: She suddenly burst into tears. / Out of nowhere, she started crying.
examples.note: 急に → 'suddenly / all of a sudden / out of nowhere'

usage_rules: な-adj + に → English '-ly' adverb is usually the first candidate
When '-ly' form is awkward, use adverbial phrase: 静かに → 'without a sound / in silence'
急に is extremely common in JP light novels → prefer 'suddenly / all of a sudden' over 'abruptly' in casual register
穏やかに → 'gently / calmly / softly' — choose by context (physical movement vs tone of voice)

patterns.japanese_structure: な-adjective + じゃない / ではない — negative predicate
patterns.english_pattern: not [adjective] / isn't [adjective] — avoid accidental double-negative transfer from JP

examples.jp: チェスは上手ではないが、下手でもない。
examples.natural: I'm not great at chess, but I'm not bad at it either.
examples.note: Double predicate — both negatives must appear clearly in EN

examples.jp: あの先生、字があまり綺麗じゃないね。
examples.natural: That teacher's handwriting isn't very neat, is it?
examples.note: じゃない + あまり → 'not very [adj]' — hedge preserved

usage_rules: JP negative な-adj → EN 'not [adj]' or 'isn't [adj]'
Double negative in JP (上手ではない + 下手でもない) → keep BOTH negations in EN for accuracy
Watch for じゃなくて (corrective negative) → 'not [A], but [B]' restructuring pattern

patterns.japanese_structure: な-adjective + だった / でした — past tense predicate
patterns.english_pattern: was [adjective] / used to be [adjective] — match temporal nuance

examples.jp: 子供の頃、この街は静かだったけど、今はにぎやかだね。
examples.natural: This town used to be so quiet when I was little, but now it's so lively.
examples.note: だったけど = past contrast → 'used to be' emphasizes the change

examples.jp: 彼女は昔、有名だった。
examples.natural: She used to be famous. / She was famous once.
examples.note: だった alone = simple past; 昔 + だった = 'used to be'

usage_rules: Simple だった → 'was [adj]' — neutral past
昔は～だった / 以前は～だった → 'used to be [adj]' — implies no longer true
だったのに (concessive past) → 'even though [it] was / even though [she] used to be' — conveys disappointment

date_time_ordering.description: Japanese date/time formats run big-to-small (Year→Month→Day→Hour→Minute). EN runs small-to-big for dates (Month Day, Year). Time ordering is the same. Imperial calendar years require conversion or parenthetical. (Source: Tofugu Grammar — Date and Time)

patterns.japanese_structure: YYYY年MM月DD日 — Year Month Day order
patterns.english_pattern: [Month name] [DD], [YYYY] — or [Weekday], [Month name] [DD], [YYYY]

examples.jp: 2022年2月4日金曜日
examples.natural: Friday, February 4th, 2022
examples.note: Full date with weekday — JP big-to-small → EN small-to-big with weekday first

examples.jp: 3月15日
examples.natural: March 15th / the 15th of March
examples.note: No year specified — use 'March 15th' in narrative prose

usage_rules: NEVER output Japanese date order (2022/2/4) in EN prose — reorder to EN standard
In narrative prose prefer: 'March 15th' — less formal than 'the 15th of March'
When no year: 'March 15th' or 'the fifteenth of March' depending on register
Weekday: comes FIRST in EN ('Friday, March 15th') — opposite of JP order

patterns.japanese_structure: 元号 + year: 平成23年 / 昭和45年 / 令和5年 — imperial era calendar
patterns.english_pattern: Western year equivalent — or leave with parenthetical if cultural context matters

examples.jp: 平成23年
examples.natural: 2011 (the 23rd year of the Heisei era)
examples.note: For EN readers: convert to Western year; add parenthetical if era name carries narrative meaning

examples.jp: 昭和時代
examples.natural: the Showa era / the postwar era
examples.note: Era names as cultural concepts — do NOT convert to year range; use era name with gloss if needed

examples.jp: 令和元年
examples.natural: 2019 / the first year of the Reiwa era
examples.note: 元年 = first year of an era (2019 for Reiwa)

usage_rules: In contemporary novels referencing actual dates: convert to Western year in EN
When the ERA NAME itself is the cultural/emotional reference: keep the era name + optional gloss
昭和 often evokes 'nostalgic old Japan' — do not reduce to '1926–1989' without context
元年 = inaugural year — 'the first year of the [Era] era' if culturally significant

patterns.japanese_structure: ～くらい / ～頃 — approximate time/quantity marker
patterns.english_pattern: around [time] / roughly [time] / about [time] — casual approximation

examples.jp: 三時頃に会いましょう。
examples.natural: Let's meet around three.
examples.note: 頃 → 'around' — preferred in casual register

examples.jp: 一時間くらい待った。
examples.natural: I waited for about an hour. / I waited around an hour.
examples.note: くらい → 'about / around' — interchangeable with 頃 for quantities

usage_rules: くらい/頃 → 'around / about / roughly' — choose based on register
Casual dialogue: 'around three' (no 'o'clock' needed)
Narrative prose: 'around three in the afternoon / roughly an hour' — slightly more explicit

patterns.japanese_structure: ～前 / ～過ぎ — shortly before or shortly after a time
patterns.english_pattern: just before [time] / a little before [time] / just past [time] / a little after [time]

examples.jp: 五時少し前
examples.natural: just before five / a little before five
examples.note: 前 = before the hour; 少し adds 'a little / just'

examples.jp: 三時過ぎ
examples.natural: just past three / a little after three
examples.note: 過ぎ = past/after the hour

usage_rules: ～前 → 'before / just before / a little before [time]'
～過ぎ → 'past / after / just after [time]'
In narration: 'just past three in the afternoon' — adds texture vs bare time stamp

adjective_verb_sou.description: 〜そう (adjective/verb) marks speculative appearance based on visible evidence or received information. Two DISTINCT constructions: [adj stem]そう = 'looks [adj]'; [plain]そうだ = 'I heard / apparently'. Never conflate them. (Source: Tofugu Grammar — Adjective そう)

patterns.japanese_structure: [い-adj stem, drop い] + そう — speculative appearance from visual evidence
patterns.english_pattern: looks [adj] / seems [adj] / appears [adj]

examples.jp: おいしそう！
examples.natural: That looks delicious!
examples.note: おいし + そう — drop い

examples.jp: おいしそうなケーキだね。
examples.natural: That's a delicious-looking cake!
examples.note: そうな attaches to nouns as modifier

examples.jp: 楽しそうに笑っていた。
examples.natural: She was laughing like she was having fun.
examples.note: そうに = adverbial form

examples.jp: この問題、難しそう。
examples.natural: This problem looks difficult.
examples.note: Visual-based speculation

usage_rules: Formation: drop final い from い-adj → add そう (おいしい→おいしそう, 難しい→難しそう)
Special case: いい → よさそう (NOT いそう) — the stem shifts to よ
Negative: おいしくない → おいしくなさそう — insert さ before そう
After そう attaches, behaves like a な-adj: そうな + noun, そうに + verb, そうだ sentence-final
Speculative only — speaker is inferring from what they can see or imagine, NOT reporting hearsay

patterns.japanese_structure: [な-adj stem] + そう — appearance based on visible evidence
patterns.english_pattern: seems [adj] / looks [adj] / appears [adj]

examples.jp: 便利そう。
examples.natural: That looks convenient.
examples.note: 便利 (na-adj) + そう directly

examples.jp: あの人、優しそう。
examples.natural: That person looks kind.
examples.note: Character inference from appearance

examples.jp: あのアパート、狭そう。
examples.natural: That apartment looks small.
examples.note: Visual assessment

usage_rules: Formation: na-adj stem (no な) + そう directly — no modification needed
Negative na-adj: 便利じゃない → 便利じゃなさそう — insert さ before そう
Result behaves like na-adj: そうな + noun, そうに + verb, そうです (polite)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Verb stem (masu-stem)] + そう — speculative imminent action
patterns.english_pattern: looks like [someone] is about to [verb] / on the verge of [verb]ing

examples.jp: 泣きそう。
examples.natural: She looks like she's about to cry.
examples.note: 泣く → 泣き + そう — imminent action

examples.jp: 今にも倒れそうだ。
examples.natural: It looks like it's about to fall.
examples.note: 今にも intensifies imminence

examples.jp: 壊れそう。
examples.natural: It looks like it's about to break.
examples.note: 壊れる → 壊れ + そう

usage_rules: Formation: masu-stem (drop ます) + そう — 泣きます→泣き, 倒れます→倒れ
Means imminent action — speaker predicts based on visible cues
今にも〜そう intensifies to 'on the very verge of...'
LN use: '彼女は泣きそうだった' = 'She looked like she was about to cry' — NOT 'apparently she cried'
CRITICAL: Do NOT confuse with [plain form]そうだ (hearsay) — see hearsay_sou_da_distinction

patterns.japanese_structure: [Plain form (full, including い)] + そうだ — HEARSAY, not appearance
patterns.english_pattern: I heard that... / Apparently... / It seems (that) [clause]

examples.jp: あのケーキ、おいしいそうです。
examples.natural: I've heard that cake is delicious.
examples.note: おいしい (full い retained) + そう = hearsay

examples.jp: あのケーキ、おいしそうです。
examples.natural: That cake looks delicious.
examples.note: おいし (drop い) + そう = appearance — CONTRAST

examples.jp: 明日、雨が降るそうだ。
examples.natural: Apparently it will rain tomorrow.
examples.note: Plain verb + そうだ = hearsay

examples.jp: 彼女は忙しいそうだ。
examples.natural: I heard she's busy.
examples.note: Full い-adj + そうだ = hearsay

usage_rules: HEARSAY: [full plain form]そうだ — the い of い-adj is RETAINED (おいしいそうだ)
APPEARANCE: [adj stem without い]そう — the い is DROPPED (おいしそう)
The presence or absence of the final い is the only orthographic difference — extremely common mistranslation source
Hearsay そうだ = 'I heard / apparently' — speaker relaying secondhand information, NOT personally observing
For LN: character internal reactions use appearance そう; narrated reports use hearsay そうだ

topic_subject_ha_ga.description: は marks the topic (established/known info, contrast spotlight); が marks the subject (new info / exclusive identification). Both can appear in the same sentence serving different roles. Critical for sentence-level naturalness in EN translation. (Source: Tofugu Grammar — Particle は / Particle が)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Topic noun] + は — marks established/known info as theme; focus is the new predicate
patterns.english_pattern: [Topic] is/was/does [predicate] — topic becomes EN subject or is dropped

examples.jp: 日本語はおもしろい。
examples.natural: Japanese is interesting.
examples.note: は = known topic; interesting = new info

examples.jp: 東京は物価が高い。
examples.natural: Tokyo has a high cost of living.
examples.note: は spotlights Tokyo; が marks inner-clause subject

examples.jp: 夏は日本へ行くつもりです。
examples.natural: I plan to go to Japan in the summer.
examples.note: 夏 = established topic frame

usage_rules: は = spotlight on topic — what follows is new/important information
Topic is always something the speaker assumes the listener can identify from context
In EN: は-topic usually becomes the grammatical subject, or a prepositional frame ('as for / in the case of')
Contrastive は (contrast implied): '東京は物価が高い' often implies 'but not other places'
は CANNOT follow question words (誰/何/どこ) — those unknown referents use が/を etc.

patterns.japanese_structure: [Subject noun] + が — marks subject with exclusive/emphatic identification
patterns.english_pattern: [Subject] [predicate] — emphasis on WHICH one / WHO did it

examples.jp: あの犬が吠えた。
examples.natural: THAT dog barked. / That dog is the one that barked.
examples.note: が identifies the specific barking dog, excludes others

examples.jp: 私が社長です。
examples.natural: I'm the president (not them).
examples.note: が = exclusive identification in lineup

examples.jp: 誰かがいる。
examples.natural: Someone's here.
examples.note: が with new-info subject

usage_rules: が = exclusive identification — 'THIS one (not others) did it'
Use が for new information: subject not yet established in discourse
が in answer to 誰/何 questions: '誰が...？' → '私が...' — always が for identification answers
In relative clauses: main-clause は often shifts to が in subordinate context
In EN: が-emphasis can be rendered with stress, 'the one who', or italics in LN context

patterns.japanese_structure: [Noun] + が + [stative adj/verb] — が with like/dislike/want/ability verbs
patterns.english_pattern: [Subject] loves/hates/wants/can/understands [noun] — EN uses transitive structure

examples.jp: 梅干が大好き。
examples.natural: I love umeboshi.
examples.note: JP: umeboshi is lovable (subject); EN flips to transitive

examples.jp: 日本語が分かる。
examples.natural: I understand Japanese.
examples.note: JP: Japanese is comprehensible; EN: I understand it

examples.jp: 音楽が聞こえる。
examples.natural: I can hear music.
examples.note: JP: music is audible; EN: I can hear it

examples.jp: 小犬が欲しい。
examples.natural: I want a puppy.
examples.note: JP: puppy is desired; EN: I want it

usage_rules: These verbs are intransitive in JP — the 'desired/liked/known thing' is the が-subject
EN translation flips the structure: JP subject (が) → EN direct object
NEVER render as 'umeboshi loves me' — the speaker/experiencer is the invisible topic
好き/嫌い/得意/苦手 are な-adjectives — they describe the subject as 'likable/detestable'
が vs を with たい: XがYたい (spontaneous desire) vs XをYたい (more intentional, action-oriented)

patterns.japanese_structure: Same noun uses は vs が — discourse role (known vs new) determines choice
japanese_indicators: は → が switch in relative clauses
patterns.english_pattern: No particle change in EN — both render as subject; detect discourse role from context

examples.jp: 猫がいる。(first mention) → その猫は黒い。(re-mention)
examples.natural: There's a cat. The cat is black.
examples.note: First mention: が (new info); second mention: は (known topic)

examples.jp: スミスさんがお金を見つけた (relative) → スミスさんは喜んだ (main)
examples.natural: Mr. Smith, who found the money, was pleased.
examples.note: が in relative clause; は in main clause

usage_rules: FIRST MENTION of unknown referent → が (introduces new subject to discourse)
SUBSEQUENT MENTION of known referent → は (topic, established from context)
RELATIVE CLAUSES: は in main clause → が in subordinate/relative clause
CONTRAST: は when implying 'but not X, Y, Z' (contrastive spotlight)
LN translation: these nuances usually absorbed into EN sentence structure — no EN marker needed

teiru_aspect.description: 〜ている describes an 'activated ongoing situation' started in the past. For dynamic verbs: progressive (is [verb]ing). For instantaneous-change verbs: resultant state (is in the state resulting from [verb]ing). One of the most consequential JP aspect markers for LN translation. (Source: Tofugu Grammar — 〜ている)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Dynamic verb] + ている — ongoing/continuous action
patterns.english_pattern: is [verb]ing / has been [verb]ing — present progressive or present perfect progressive

examples.jp: お父さんはリビングでテレビを観ている。
examples.natural: Dad is watching TV in the living room.
examples.note: Dynamic verb 観る → progressive

examples.jp: 去年から日本語を勉強している。
examples.natural: I've been studying Japanese since last year.
examples.note: Duration → present perfect continuous

examples.jp: 最近よく納豆を食べている。
examples.natural: I've been eating natto a lot lately.
examples.note: Habitual frequency → present perfect continuous

examples.jp: 母は今、買い物に行っています。
examples.natural: Mom is out shopping now.
examples.note: 行っている = ongoing state of having left (resultant departure)

usage_rules: Dynamic verbs (食べる/観る/勉強する): ている = is [verb]ing (present progressive)
With duration markers (去年から/ずっと): ている = has been [verb]ing (present perfect continuous)
Casual register: ている → てる in dialogue — render as contracted EN when appropriate
Habitual ている with 最近/いつも: 'has been [verb]ing lately' or 'always [verb]s'
行っている = gone out (resultant state of leaving — cross-reference teiru_resultant_state)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Instantaneous-change verb] + ている — resultant state
patterns.english_pattern: is [state adj] / has [past participle] — NOT 'is [verb]ing'

examples.jp: テレビがついている。
examples.natural: The TV is on.
examples.note: つく = turn on (instant) → ている = is currently on; NOT 'turning on'

examples.jp: 虫が死んでいる。
examples.natural: The bug is dead.
examples.note: 死ぬ = die (instant) → ている = is dead; NOT 'is dying'

examples.jp: 結婚している。
examples.natural: She is married.
examples.note: 結婚する (instant transition) → ている = married state

examples.jp: ドアが開いている。
examples.natural: The door is open.
examples.note: 開く = open (instant) → ている = is open

examples.jp: 知っている。
examples.natural: I know.
examples.note: 知る = come to know → ている = know (current state of having learned)

usage_rules: If the base verb describes INSTANT change (die/open/turn on/get married/know): ている = RESULTANT STATE
NEVER render instantaneous-change ている as progressive: 死んでいる ≠ 'is dying', 知っている ≠ 'is knowing'
Key test: can the action take sustained time? Yes → progressive; No (instant state flip) → resultant state
知っている = 'know' (always resultant state in EN); 知らない = 'don't know'
結婚している = 'is married'; やせている = 'is slim' (not 'is losing weight')

patterns.japanese_structure: [Verb] + ている with frequency/habitual context markers
patterns.english_pattern: always [verb]s / generally [verb]s / has been [verb]ing — habitual/recurring

examples.jp: 毎朝納豆を食べている。
examples.natural: I eat natto every morning.
examples.note: Established habit: simple present

examples.jp: 私は東京に住んでいる。
examples.natural: I live in Tokyo.
examples.note: 住む is always ている for current residence in JP

examples.jp: 最近よく走っている。
examples.natural: I've been running a lot lately.
examples.note: Recent habit: present perfect continuous

usage_rules: 住んでいる = 'live in [place]' — ALWAYS ている in JP for current residence (temporary or permanent)
Habitual ている = simple present in EN for established routines
With recently-started habits (最近〜ている): 'has been [verb]ing lately'
Proactive habitual ている (vs plain form) adds nuance 'actively/willingly' doing the habit — usually absorbed in EN

patterns.japanese_structure: [Verb] + ていた — past ongoing or past resultant state
patterns.english_pattern: was [verb]ing / had been [verb]ing / was [state adj] — past progressive or past state

examples.jp: 彼女は笑っていた。
examples.natural: She was laughing.
examples.note: Dynamic verb + ていた = past progressive

examples.jp: ドアが開いていた。
examples.natural: The door was open.
examples.note: Instantaneous-change verb + ていた = past resultant state

examples.jp: ずっと勉強していた。
examples.natural: I had been studying the whole time.
examples.note: Duration + ていた = past perfect continuous

examples.jp: 知っていた。
examples.natural: I knew.
examples.note: 知る resultant ていた = past state of knowing

usage_rules: Same progressive vs resultant-state rules as ている, but shifted to past tense
Dynamic verb + ていた = was [verb]ing (past progressive)
Instantaneous-change verb + ていた = was [state adj] / had been [state adj]
With duration (ずっと/長い間): 'had been [verb]ing' (past perfect continuous) for LN narrative depth
知っていた = 'knew / had known'; 死んでいた = 'was dead / lay dead'

iru_aru_existence.description: いる and ある both mean 'to be / to exist / there is/are'. Choice determined by whether the thing is expected to move (いる) or not (ある). Animate beings → いる; inanimate objects, plants, abstract concepts, events → ある. (Source: Tofugu Grammar — いる・ある)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Animate noun] + が/に + いる — existence of animate/mobile beings
patterns.english_pattern: there is/are [person/animal] / [person/animal] is [location]

examples.jp: 川の中に魚がいる。
examples.natural: There are fish in the river.
examples.note: Fish are animate → いる

examples.jp: 誰かがいる。
examples.natural: Someone's here.
examples.note: Person → いる

examples.jp: オフィスに弁護士と依頼人がいる。
examples.natural: There is a lawyer and a client in the office.
examples.note: Even motionless people → いる (movement always possible)

usage_rules: いる = use for people, animals, insects, fish, monsters, spirits — anything that can self-propel
Even if currently motionless (sleeping, comatose): いる (potential for movement)
Plants/trees → ある (cannot self-propel despite biological life)
In EN: render as 'there is/are' or '[location] has [creature]' depending on register

patterns.japanese_structure: [Inanimate noun] + が/に + ある — existence of inanimate objects, plants, abstract
patterns.english_pattern: there is/are [thing] / [thing] is [location] / has [thing]

examples.jp: 庭にはたくさんの花と木がある。
examples.natural: There are many flowers and trees in the yard.
examples.note: Plants → ある

examples.jp: 美しい魚の絵がある。
examples.natural: There is a beautiful painting of fish.
examples.note: The painting (inanimate) → ある, not the depicted fish

examples.jp: この国には法律がある。
examples.natural: There are laws in this country.
examples.note: Abstract concept → ある

examples.jp: テーブルの上に照り焼きにした魚がある。
examples.natural: There's teriyaki fish on the table.
examples.note: Dead/cooked fish → ある (no longer expected to move)

usage_rules: ある = use for inanimate physical objects, plants, abstract concepts (laws, rules, ideas, words)
A painting of a fish uses ある — talking about the painting itself, not the depicted animal
A cooked/dead fish → ある (no longer expected to move)
Abstract nouns: rules, plans, events, experiences, facts → always ある

patterns.japanese_structure: Mobile inanimate object (vehicle/robot/elevator): いる when mobility focused, ある when location focused
patterns.english_pattern: both render as 'is [location]' or 'there is [thing]' in EN — no distinction in EN output

examples.jp: バス、まだバス停にいる！
examples.natural: The bus is still at the bus stop!
examples.note: Driver inside, expecting movement → いる

examples.jp: バスは車庫の中にある。
examples.natural: There are buses in the depot.
examples.note: Stationary with no movement expectation → ある

examples.jp: 今、エレベーターは三階にいる。
examples.natural: The elevator is on the 3rd floor right now.
examples.note: Focus on mobility/motion → いる

usage_rules: Mobile machines (buses, elevators, Roombas): いる when focusing on movement/mobility expectation
Same objects: ある when treating them as fixtures or stating simple location without mobility nuance
EN translation: both いる and ある for mobile objects render identically — no EN distinction needed
LN use: いる for a vehicle about to *dramatically* depart can be captured with urgency words in EN

patterns.japanese_structure: [Person に] + は + [thing] + が + ある — possession / abstract existence
patterns.english_pattern: has [thing] / there is [event] / have [experience] — possession/abstract existence

examples.jp: 私（に）は夢がある。
examples.natural: I have a dream.
examples.note: Abstract possession: dream exists for me

examples.jp: その日はもう予定がある。
examples.natural: I already have plans that day.
examples.note: Event/plan → ある

examples.jp: そのお祭り、行ったことがあるよ。
examples.natural: I've been to that festival before.
examples.note: 〜たことがある = have [done] before — experience as ある

examples.jp: 町のお祭りがあるんだ。
examples.natural: There's a town festival happening.
examples.note: Event existence → ある

usage_rules: ある for: events (祭り), plans (予定), experiences (〜たことがある), rules/laws, abstract concepts
Possession pattern: [person]に + [thing]が + ある = 'person has thing' (thing is the subject doing the 'existing')
〜たことがある = 'have [done] before' — こと (experience) + ある always
予定がある = 'have plans' — events that don't physically move use ある

sugiru_excess.description: 〜すぎる follows verb stems or adj stems to mean 'too much / excessively'. In positive contexts, often means 'SO [adj]!' (expressive hyperbole common in LN dialogue). Conjugates as ichidan verb. (Source: Tofugu Grammar — すぎる)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Verb stem / い-adj (drop い) / な-adj stem] + すぎる — excessive degree
patterns.english_pattern: too [adj] / [verb]ed too much / overly [adj]

examples.jp: 食べすぎた。
examples.natural: I ate too much.
examples.note: 食べる → 食べ + すぎた

examples.jp: 暑すぎる！
examples.natural: It's too hot!
examples.note: 暑い → 暑 + すぎる (drop い)

examples.jp: 静かすぎる。
examples.natural: It's too quiet.
examples.note: 静か (な-adj) + すぎる directly

examples.jp: 寝すぎた。
examples.natural: I slept too much.
examples.note: 寝る → 寝 + すぎた

usage_rules: Verb: masu-stem + すぎる (食べます→食べすぎる, 飲みます→飲みすぎる)
い-adj: drop final い + すぎる (暑い→暑すぎる, 難しい→難しすぎる)
な-adj: stem (no な) + すぎる (有名→有名すぎる, 静か→静かすぎる)
Special: いい → よすぎる (NOT いすぎる) — stem shifts to よ
Negative excess: すぎる conjugates normally — すぎない/すぎた/すぎて

patterns.japanese_structure: [adj/verb] + すぎる — positive expressive hyperbole (LN/dialogue common)
patterns.english_pattern: SO [adj]! / [adj] as hell! / ridiculously [adj] / way too [adj] (positive hyperbole)

examples.jp: 安すぎる！
examples.natural: It's SO cheap! / That's ridiculously cheap!
examples.note: Positive context: cheap + unexpected = expressive amazement

examples.jp: 可愛すぎる！
examples.natural: She's SO cute! / Ugh, she's too cute!
examples.note: Common in LN character reactions

examples.jp: お得すぎる！
examples.natural: What a deal! / That's such a good deal!
examples.note: Positive excess — amazement, not complaint

usage_rules: すぎる is MORE NEUTRAL than English 'too' — commonly expresses positive hyperbole in casual/excited speech
Context determines negative vs positive: 'ate too much' (bad) vs 'SO cute' (good)
LN character reactions: すぎる in exclamations is often positive expressive — match with 'SO', 'ridiculously', 'way too', 'absolutely'
Register: すぎ (without る) is more casual/sentence-final: 'かっこよすぎ！' = 'he's SO cool!'

patterns.japanese_structure: [Neg form ending ない] → drop い + さ + すぎる = なさすぎる — negation past threshold
patterns.english_pattern: not [adj/verb] at ALL / barely [verb]s at all / so incredibly un-[adj]

examples.jp: 面白くなさすぎる！
examples.natural: It's not funny at all! / So incredibly unfunny!
examples.note: 面白くない → 面白くなさ + すぎる

examples.jp: うちの子は野菜を食べなさすぎる！
examples.natural: Our kid basically doesn't eat vegetables at all!
examples.note: 食べない → 食べなさ + すぎる

examples.jp: わからなさすぎて困る。
examples.natural: I'm so lost it's not even funny.
examples.note: 分からない → わからなさすぎる — subjective frustration

usage_rules: Formation: [negative verb/adj ending in ない] → drop final い → add さすぎる
Meaning: the degree of NOT doing exceeds an implied baseline — more expressive than 全然〜ない
More subjective and humorous tone than objective 全然〜ない negation
EN: 'not X at all' / 'so incredibly un-X' / 'barely X in the slightest' — match register

patterns.japanese_structure: 〜すぎ (drop る) — noun form meaning 'the act/state of being excessively [verb/adj]'
patterns.english_pattern: [verb]ing too much / overindulgence in [verb]ing / too much [noun]

examples.jp: 食べすぎは体に良くない。
examples.natural: Eating too much isn't good for you.
examples.note: 食べすぎ = the act of overeating — noun as topic

examples.jp: 飲みすぎで頭が痛い。
examples.natural: I have a headache from drinking too much.
examples.note: 飲みすぎ = the excess drinking — cause marker で

examples.jp: いつも食べすぎだよ。
examples.natural: You always eat too much.
examples.note: 食べすぎ + だ — nominalized state as predicate

usage_rules: 〜すぎ (stem, without る) = noun: '食べすぎ' = 'the overeating / eating too much'
Can be topic (は/も), cause marker (で), or predicate (〜すぎだ/です)
〜すぎです (nominal + copula) = more casual; 〜すぎます (verbal polite) = describes the action itself
EN: gerund construction works well — 'eating too much is [...]', 'from sleeping too much'

iku_kuru_directionality.description: 行く = move away from speaker's current position; 来る = move toward speaker's current position.
