JP is speaker-position anchored; EN 'come/go' can be listener-anchored. Critical for LN scene perspective consistency — the same physical movement requires different EN words depending on narrative POV. (Source: Tofugu Grammar — 行く・来る)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Motion] + 行く — movement away from speaker's current position
patterns.english_pattern: go / went / leave / left — movement away from current viewpoint

examples.jp: 電車、行っちゃったね。
examples.natural: The train's gone.
examples.note: Train left the speaker's location

examples.jp: ちょっと待って！今行くよ！
examples.natural: Hold on, I'm coming now!
examples.note: Speaker says 行く (going away from self) but EN uses 'coming' (listener-anchored)

examples.jp: 友達とランチ食べてくる。
examples.natural: I'm going out for lunch with my friends.
examples.note: 行ってくる compound — leaving and returning

usage_rules: 行く = movement AWAY from speaker — always speaker-position anchored
CRITICAL: JP '今行くよ' (I'm going now) = EN 'I'm coming' — completely opposite word choice because EN is listener-anchored
行っている = has gone out (resultant state of having departed)
行ってきます = 'I'm going out (and will return)' — standard departure greeting
In LN narration: 行く when the POV character is the one leaving or watching a departure

patterns.japanese_structure: [Motion] + 来る — movement toward speaker's current position
patterns.english_pattern: come / came / arrive / approach — movement toward current viewpoint

examples.jp: 電車、もうすぐ来るよ。
examples.natural: The train will come soon.
examples.note: Train approaching speaker's location

examples.jp: いつか会いに来てね。
examples.natural: Come visit me someday!
examples.note: Friend requests movement toward her future position

examples.jp: 今ニューヨークに来ているんです。
examples.natural: I'm in New York now.
examples.note: 来ている = resultant state of having arrived here

usage_rules: 来る = movement TOWARD speaker (or toward speaker's expected future position)
来ている = currently present at a location after arriving (resultant state of coming)
Family awaiting someone's return: use 来る — movement is perceived as arriving toward them
〜てくれる vs 〜てくる: 教えてくる = action 'comes at me' (pushy/unsolicited); 教えてくれる = does X for me (grateful)

patterns.japanese_structure: Same physical action: 行く vs 来る depend on which side of movement the narrator is on
japanese_indicators: 〜に行く vs 〜に来る for same destination
入ってきた vs 入っていった
patterns.english_pattern: go vs come — EN must match narrative viewpoint, not literal JP word

examples.jp: 花子は日本に帰っていった。(narrator watching her leave)
examples.natural: Hanako left for Japan.
examples.note: いく = away from narrator; EN 'left for' captures departure perspective

examples.jp: 花子は来月日本に帰ってくる。(family in Japan)
examples.natural: Hanako will come back to Japan next month.
examples.note: くる = toward family; EN 'come back'

examples.jp: 先生が教室に入ってきた。
examples.natural: The teacher came into the classroom.
examples.note: くる = teacher moved toward students (narrator is inside classroom)

examples.jp: 先生が教室から出ていった。
examples.natural: The teacher went out of the classroom.
examples.note: いく = teacher moved away from narrator (narrator inside)

usage_rules: EN translator must determine: where is the narrative POV anchored?
If POV is with the departure point: 行く → 'go / leave / head out'
If POV is with the arrival point: 来る → 'come / arrive / approach'
入ってきた = 'came in' (narrator inside); 入っていった = 'went in' (narrator outside)
Consistent POV anchoring throughout an LN chapter = consistent go/come — do NOT mix without an intentional perspective shift

patterns.japanese_structure: [Verb て] + いく/くる — auxiliary directional aspect (action + direction, or gradual process)
patterns.english_pattern: varying — accompaniment (take/bring), motion (enter/exit), or gradual process aspect

examples.jp: ドーナツを買っていくよ！
examples.natural: I'll get some donuts for you!
examples.note: 買って + いく = buy then go; EN naturalizes by dropping 'and go'

examples.jp: ドーナツを買ってきたよ！
examples.natural: I got some donuts for you!
examples.note: 買って + くる = bought then came; EN naturalizes

examples.jp: 先生が教室に入ってきた。
examples.natural: The teacher came into the classroom.
examples.note: 入って + くる = enter toward speaker

examples.jp: 一緒に頑張っていきましょう。
examples.natural: Let's keep working on this together!
examples.note: いく = gradual process moving forward from now

examples.jp: ずっと頑張ってきた。
examples.natural: I've been working hard this whole time.
examples.note: くる = gradual process ending at now (retrospective)

usage_rules: 〜ていく: action + movement away, OR gradual process starting from now going forward
〜てくる: action + movement toward, OR gradual process ending at now (retrospective completion)
Accompaniment: 持っていく = 'take'; 持ってくる = 'bring'; 連れていく = 'take someone'; 連れてくる = 'bring someone'
Gradual ていく: '〜していきましょう' = 'let's continue to do this' — forward-looking process marker
Gradual てくる: '〜してきた' = 'has been doing up to now' — retrospective completion marker
In EN: accompaniment forms naturalize to single verb ('take/bring'); gradual forms → 'keep [verb]ing' / 'have been [verb]ing'

adj_sa_mi_nominalizers.description: 〜さ turns adjectives into objective measurable nouns (like '-ness'); 〜み turns adjectives into subjective experiential nouns. Both critical for elegant EN noun phrasing in prose. (Source: Tofugu — Adjective さ / Adjective み)

patterns.japanese_structure: [い-adj stem / な-adj stem] + さ — objective measurable noun
patterns.english_pattern: [adj]ness / the [adj]ity of / how [adj] — objective quality as noun

examples.jp: カバンの重さを測る
examples.natural: measure the weight of the bag
examples.note: 重さ = weight (objective, measurable)

examples.jp: プールの深さ
examples.natural: the depth of the pool
examples.note: 深さ = depth

examples.jp: テストの難しさ
examples.natural: the difficulty of the test / how difficult the test is
examples.note: 難しさ = difficulty (presentable as objective)

examples.jp: 大きさ
examples.natural: size (in general)
examples.note: positive pole of opposite pair represents the whole concept

usage_rules: 〜さ = objective, measurable quality — like '-ness'/'-ity' in EN
い-adjectives: drop い, add さ (高い→高さ, 深い→深さ, 難しい→難しさ)
な-adjectives: drop な, add さ (便利→便利さ, 有名→有名さ)
Positive member of opposite pair represents whole concept: 大きさ = size (not bigness), 速さ = speed (not fastness)
With 〜やすい/〜にくい/〜たい: chain works — 使いやすさ (ease of use), 行きたさ (degree of wanting to go)

patterns.japanese_structure: [selected い/な-adj stem] + み — subjective experiential noun
patterns.english_pattern: a [adj] quality / a sense of [adj]ness / [adj]ness (personally felt)

examples.jp: あの映画には重みがある
examples.natural: That movie carries weight / has gravitas
examples.note: 重み = weight (metaphorical, felt meaning)

examples.jp: 前田さんには温かみがある
examples.natural: There's a warmth to Maeda / Maeda has warmth
examples.note: 温かみ = warmth (personal perceived quality)

examples.jp: 楽しみにしている
examples.natural: I'm looking forward to it
examples.note: 楽しみ = the joy/anticipation (subjective internal state)

examples.jp: 面白みに欠ける
examples.natural: lacking in charm / without much appeal
examples.note: 面白み = appeal (personal, vs 面白さ which is more objective)

usage_rules: 〜み = subjective, personally-experienced quality — choose over さ when the feeling matters more than the measurement
NOT all adjectives take み — mostly emotion adjectives and sensory adjectives
Many み-nouns have む-verb origins: 楽しむ→楽しみ, 悲しむ→悲しみ, 痛む→痛み
Flavor mi nouns often written with 味 kanji: 甘み/甘味, 旨み/旨味
面白み (subjective appeal) vs 面白さ (objectively funny amount) — choose based on whether it's in the eye of the beholder

chuu_juu_naka_readings.description: 中 has three readings critical for LN translation: じゅう (throughout/all over), ちゅう (in the middle of/during), なか (inside/among/under circumstances). Misreading creates completely wrong meaning. (Source: Tofugu — 中)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Location/time period] + 中（じゅう） — throughout / all over / all through
patterns.english_pattern: throughout [place/time] / all over [place] / all [time] long / the whole [time]

examples.jp: 家中掃除する
examples.natural: clean the whole house / clean throughout the house
examples.note: 家中 = all over the house

examples.jp: 一晩中ゲームをしていた
examples.natural: I played games all night / I played games the whole night
examples.note: 一晩中 = all night long

examples.jp: 夏休み中ずっと家にいた
examples.natural: I stayed home all through summer vacation
examples.note: なつやすみじゅう = all through; ちゅう = merely during

examples.jp: 世界中で有名
examples.natural: famous all over the world / world-renowned
examples.note: 世界中 = throughout the world

usage_rules: 中じゅう = scope/coverage — 'everything everywhere within that place/period'
With location: 'all over / throughout / everywhere in [place]'
With time: 'all [time] / throughout [period] / from start to finish of [time]'
年中 (ねんじゅう) = 'all the time / constantly' (evolved meaning); 一年中 = literally 'all year round'
DISTINGUISH: 夏休み中じゅう (all summer) vs 夏休み中ちゅう (during summer vacation)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Activity noun] + 中（ちゅう） — in the middle of / during / currently [action]ing
patterns.english_pattern: in the middle of [noun] / during [noun] / currently [verb]ing / [noun] in progress

examples.jp: 田中は今、会議中です
examples.natural: Tanaka is in a meeting right now.
examples.note: 会議中 = in the middle of a meeting

examples.jp: 食事中は静かにしましょう
examples.natural: Please keep quiet during the meal.
examples.note: 食事中 = during the meal

examples.jp: 検討中
examples.natural: Still under consideration / currently under review
examples.note: mental activity status sign

examples.jp: 営業中
examples.natural: Open for Business
examples.note: 公共 sign use

usage_rules: 中ちゅう follows a noun (verbal/する noun) NOT a verb form — 勉強中 ⭕ / 勉強する中 ❌
For verb +ing contexts use 〜ている時/間 instead
Signs: 会議中 = Meeting in Progress, 営業中 = Open, 清掃中 = Cleaning in Progress
午前中 = 'in the morning / morning hours' (special fixed expression)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Noun] + の + 中（なか） / 〜の中で/から/に — inside / among / under circumstances
patterns.english_pattern: inside [noun] / among [noun] / in the midst of [noun] / out of [group] — varies by particle

examples.jp: 箱の中に入れる
examples.natural: put it inside the box
examples.note: の中に = inside (location)

examples.jp: 三人の中で一番背が高い
examples.natural: the tallest of the three
examples.note: の中で = out of / among (superlative)

examples.jp: 三つの中から一つ選ぶ
examples.natural: choose one from among the three
examples.note: の中から = from among

examples.jp: 嵐の中を歩いた
examples.natural: I walked through/in the middle of the storm
examples.note: circumstances: in the midst of [difficult condition]

usage_rules: 中なか + に = location (inside): 'in the car / inside the box'
中なか + で = action location (in/among): superlatives '〜の中で一番' = 'the most [adj] of [group]'
中なか + から = selection from inside: 'from among [options]'
中なか + には = 'some of [group]' — partial membership
Circumstances pattern: [難しい状況]の中 = 'in the midst of [adversity]' — common LN scene-setting

dake_restriction_particle.description: だけ restricts or limits something to a specific extent ('only / just'). Neutral valence — can express sufficiency or insufficiency. Contrasts with しか (always negative, emphasizes insufficiency), のみ (formal/official), and たったの (emphasizes smallness vs expectation). (Source: Tofugu — だけ)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Noun/adj/verb] + だけ — limits/restricts to that alone
patterns.english_pattern: only [X] / just [X] / nothing but [X] / that's all

examples.jp: 最近は漢字だけ勉強している
examples.natural: Lately I've only been studying kanji.
examples.note: restricts study topic

examples.jp: 毎日一時間だけ勉強している
examples.natural: I study for just one hour every day.
examples.note: restricts duration

examples.jp: 行くだけだよ
examples.natural: We're only going (and that's all).
examples.note: verb + だけ = limits action

examples.jp: それだけ？
examples.natural: Is that all? / That's it?
examples.note: それだけ = that amount / that alone

usage_rules: な-adj: add な before だけ — 有名なだけ (just famous)
Verb: plain form before だけ — 行くだけ; NOT polite form (❌行きますだけ)
Position matters: 漢字だけを勉強 = study ONLY kanji; 漢字を勉強するだけ = ONLY studying kanji (limits the action)
〜だけで = 'just by [verb]ing' — 想像するだけで = just by imagining it
だけでなく〜も = 'not only X but also Y' (casual: だけじゃなく)

patterns.japanese_structure: だけ vs しか (always+negative) — neutral limit vs emphatic insufficiency
patterns.english_pattern: だけ = 'only X' (neutral); しか = 'only X' (subjective complaint — X is not enough)

examples.jp: チョコレートが一つだけある
examples.natural: There is only one chocolate. (neutral statement)
examples.note: だけ = neutral observation

examples.jp: チョコレートが一つしかない
examples.natural: There's only one chocolate! (not enough!)
examples.note: しか = complaint/dissatisfaction

examples.jp: 駅まで歩いて5分しかかからない
examples.natural: It only takes 5 minutes to walk to the station! (surprisingly close)
examples.note: しか negative but POSITIVE feeling here — subjective emphasis

usage_rules: しか ALWAYS takes negative predicate — しかない / しかできない / しかいない
だけ is neutral/objective; しか adds subjective emotional charge (frustration, surprise, delight)
When the 'small amount' is a GOOD thing, しか can express delighted surprise (only 5 min!)
In LN translation: match the emotional register — しか warrants exclamation or emphasis in EN

patterns.japanese_structure: 〜だけでなく〜も — not only X but also Y
patterns.english_pattern: not only [X] but also [Y] / [X] and [Y] both / as well as

examples.jp: お肉だけでなく野菜も食べましょう
examples.natural: Let's eat not only meat but also our vegetables.
examples.note: additive with negation of limit

examples.jp: 彼は頭がいいだけでなく運動もできる
examples.natural: Not only is he smart, he's also athletic.
examples.note: quality + additional quality

usage_rules: 〜だけでなく〜も = standard 'not only X but also Y'
Casual equivalent: だけじゃなく〜も
EN options: 'not only...but also', 'as well as', 'in addition to', 'beyond just'

nda_ndesu_explanatory.description: んだ・んです (= のだ/のです) adds explanatory tone — fills gap in understanding between speakers, provides context for a request, expresses realizations, or adds authority to commands. Critical for naturalizing LN dialogue. (Source: Tofugu — んだ・んです)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Plain form verb/adj] + んだ / [noun/な-adj stem] + なんだ — explains context filling information gap
patterns.english_pattern: No direct EN particle — absorb as 'the thing is... / actually... / it's because...' or naturalize via sentence restructuring

examples.jp: 実はイタリアで育ったんだ
examples.natural: The thing is, I actually grew up in Italy.
examples.note: fills gap: explains why speaker speaks Italian

examples.jp: 同じレストランで働いてたんだ
examples.natural: It turns out we worked at the same restaurant.
examples.note: provides context for recognition

examples.jp: ベジタリアンなんだ
examples.natural: The thing is, I'm a vegetarian.
examples.note: noun/な-adj: insert な before んだ

examples.jp: 風邪ひいちゃったんだ。だから今日は会えない
examples.natural: I caught a cold — that's why I can't see you today.
examples.note: sets up explanation before consequence

usage_rules: んだ adds 'context/reason' nuance — the statement explains something the listener was wondering about
Formation: plain verb/い-adj directly + んだ; noun/な-adj + なんだ (な is essential)
Negative: ないんだ / じゃないんだ; Past: たんだ / だったんだ
EN translation: 'The thing is...' / 'Actually...' / 'It's [because]...' / restructure as causal clause
Do NOT add 'is that' mechanically — rephrase to sound natural in EN
Register: んだ = casual; んです = polite; のだ = assertive/formal writing; のです = stiff formal

patterns.japanese_structure: [Statement] + んだ — realization tone (processing new information)
patterns.english_pattern: Oh, you [verb]ed! / So you're a [noun]! / Ah, I see — [statement] / (So that's why...)

examples.jp: 昇進したんだ。おめでとう！
examples.natural: Oh, you got promoted! Congratulations!
examples.note: processing new info = realization exclamation

examples.jp: そっか、フラれちゃったんだ…
examples.natural: Ohh... so you got rejected, huh...
examples.note: self-directed realization; trailing off emotionally

examples.jp: 昇進したんですか。おめでとうございます！
examples.natural: So you got promoted? Congratulations!
examples.note: polite confirmation question

usage_rules: Realization んだ = speaker is processing new/surprising information
In dialogue: 'Oh, so...' / 'I see...' / 'So that's [why/what]...' depending on context
Self-directed (no listener): trailing realization, often with '...' — render as internal musing
んだ (plain/casual) vs んです (semi-polite) — match register in EN dialogue

patterns.japanese_structure: [Verb] + んだ / んだよ — emphatic instruction/command (dramatic or parental)
patterns.english_pattern: Run! / [Verb]! (dramatic urgency) / You [verb], okay? (gentle parental instruction)

examples.jp: 逃げるんだ！
examples.natural: Run! / Get out of here!
examples.note: dramatic movie: conveys understanding of life-threatening reality

examples.jp: ちゃんと毎日朝ごはん食べるんだよ
examples.natural: Make sure you eat breakfast every day, okay?
examples.note: parental concern-instruction; よ softens

examples.jp: 諦めるんじゃない！
examples.natural: Don't give up!
examples.note: negative command form

usage_rules: Dramatic んだ command: implies 'you need to understand the gravity of this — so [verb]!'
Parental/instructional んだよ: softer, caring — 'make sure you...' / '...okay?' in EN
Negative: 〜するんじゃない = 'Don't do that!' / '..., got it?'
In LN action scenes: simple imperative usually best — 'Run!' 'Fight!' 'Get down!'

tagaru_third_person_desire.description: 〜たがる expresses that a 3rd person is showing observable signs of wanting to do something. たい can only express 1st-person desire; たがる projects desire onto observed others. (Source: Tofugu — 〜たがる)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Verb たい stem, drop い] + がる — showing signs of wanting to do (3rd person)
patterns.english_pattern: wants to [verb] / is eager to [verb] / keeps wanting to [verb] / shows no interest in [verb]ing

examples.jp: うちの犬はいつも外に出たがる！
examples.natural: My dog always wants to go outside!
examples.note: たがる = observable wanting behavior

examples.jp: 木村さんは食べたがっている
examples.natural: Kimura wants to eat right now.
examples.note: たがっている = currently showing signs of wanting (momentary)

examples.jp: 走りたがらない
examples.natural: doesn't want to run / shows no interest in running
examples.note: negative: たがらない

usage_rules: Formation: masu-stem + たい → drop い → add がる (食べたい→食べたがる)
Simple present たがる = general tendency ('always wants to')
Continuous たがっている = wanting right now in the moment
Negative: たがらない = doesn't show signs of wanting (not たくない which is 1st-person)
NEVER use たい for observable 3rd-person desire in narration — use たがる or 〜たそうだ
Similar logic: 〜がる with adjectives (痛い→痛がる = shows signs of being in pain)

jibun_reflexive.description: 自分 = 'self' — can be reflexive (himself/herself), intensive (by oneself), or possessive (one's own). Unlike English reflexive pronouns, 自分 can refer across clause boundaries and even function as a 1st/2nd person pronoun in some registers. (Source: Tofugu — 自分)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Subject] + 自分 — reflexive (himself/herself/myself/themselves)
patterns.english_pattern: himself / herself / themselves / myself / yourself — matching subject's person/gender

examples.jp: キョーコは自分を信じている
examples.natural: Kyoko believes in herself.
examples.note: self-reference back to subject = reflexive pronoun

examples.jp: 自分を信じて
examples.natural: Believe in yourself.
examples.note: unspecified person: 'yourself'

examples.jp: 自分に自信がない
examples.natural: I lack confidence in myself.
examples.note: subject omitted; 自分 carries it from context

usage_rules: 自分 refers back to the subject of the clause — determine person from context
Can refer to a subject in a DIFFERENT clause (unlike EN reflexives which are same-clause only)
マミは、カナエが自分を責めてると言った — 自分 = Mami OR Kanae (ambiguous; use narrative context to determine)
In EN: 'himself/herself/themselves/myself/yourself/itself' — match the antecedent

patterns.japanese_structure: 自分で — by oneself / for oneself / on one's own
patterns.english_pattern: by herself / by himself / on his/her own / himself/herself (intensive after verb)

examples.jp: キョーコは家を自分で建てた
examples.natural: Kyoko built the house herself. / Kyoko built the house on her own.
examples.note: 自分で = intensive — she did it

examples.jp: 自分たちでビザの申請をした
examples.natural: We filed a visa application ourselves.
examples.note: 自分たち = plural reflexive

usage_rules: 自分で = 'by oneself' — emphasizes who did it, not necessarily 'alone without help'
For 'alone without any outside help': use 一人で (by oneself, solo)
自分で can indicate agency/initiative: 'she herself chose to...' / 'took it upon herself to...'

patterns.japanese_structure: 自分の — one's own [noun]
patterns.english_pattern: her/his/their/one's own [noun]

examples.jp: 自分の意見を言う
examples.natural: state her own opinion / speak her mind
examples.note: 自分の = one's own

examples.jp: 本当の自分を探す旅
examples.natural: a journey to find one's true self / a journey to find who she really is
examples.note: 本当の自分 = true self — identity concept

usage_rules: 自分の = possessive tied to the subject's identity — 'their own / one's own'
自分の家 = 'their own home' (not just 'their home') — emphasis on ownership/belonging
在る idioms: 自分を殺す = suppressing oneself; 自分を持つ = being self-assured; 自分を見失う = feeling lost

ga_kedo_conjunctive.description: が and けど are conjunctive particles meaning 'but' for contrast, or linkage providing context. が = more formal, appropriate for writing; けど = colloquial. Both can end sentences (trailing けど...) to soften, invite response, or imply continuation. (Source: Tofugu — が・けど)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Clause A] + が/けど/けれど + [Clause B] — contrast ('but')
patterns.english_pattern: but / however / yet / though / although / while

examples.jp: 白ワインは好きですが、赤ワインはあまり好きじゃない
examples.natural: I like white wine, but I'm not so into red.
examples.note: が = formal contrast

examples.jp: 雨が降っているけど、子供たちは外で遊んでいる
examples.natural: It's raining, but the kids are playing outside.
examples.note: けど = casual contrast

examples.jp: 母さんはコンサートに来たが、姉は来なかった
examples.natural: My mother came, but my older sister didn't.
examples.note: formal register で

usage_rules: Formality: けれども > けれど > けども > けど (least formal); が = formal/writing
な-adjectives and nouns need だ/です before が/けど: 夏ですが > 夏だけど
In LN prose/narration: が preferred; dialogue: けど natural for casual speakers
EN: 'but' for direct contrast; 'though/although' for softer; 'while' for simultaneous contrast

patterns.japanese_structure: [Statement/request] + けど / が (sentence-final) — softening, implying continuation
patterns.english_pattern: I was wondering if... / Actually, ... (trailing) / I don't mean to impose, but...

examples.jp: もしもし。予約したいんですけど…
examples.natural: Hello... I'd like to make a reservation, if possible.
examples.note: sentence-final けど opens space for listener response

examples.jp: 財布を忘れてしまったんですが、500円貸してもらえませんか
examples.natural: I forgot my wallet — would you mind lending me 500 yen?
examples.note: が sets context before the actual request

usage_rules: Sentence-final けど/が = indirect, polite — invites listener to fill in the rest
Often paired with んだ/んです for context-setting before requests: X + んですが/んですけど + [request]
Trailing '...' after けど signals the speaker is leaving the implicit 'please help me' unsaid
Phone calls often: 〜ですが (identifying self, transitioning to purpose) — render as 'This is [name]...'

mada_mou_continuation.description: まだ = 'still' (positive) / 'not yet' (negative) — the situation hasn't passed a reference point. もう = 'already/anymore' — the situation HAS passed a reference point. The interplay between them is rich in LN character inner thought and dialogue. (Source: Tofugu — まだ)

patterns.japanese_structure: まだ + [positive predicate] — still (situation continues)
patterns.english_pattern: still [verb/adj] / still [present] / [situation] continues

examples.jp: まだ食べてる！
examples.natural: I'm still eating!
examples.note: continuation past implied endpoint

examples.jp: まだ先生
examples.natural: Still a teacher.
examples.note: still holds that role

examples.jp: 雨はまだ降り続けるだろう
examples.natural: The rain will probably still keep falling.
examples.note: future continuation

examples.jp: まだまだ頑張れる
examples.natural: I can still keep going / There's still plenty of fight left in me.
examples.note: まだまだ = emphatic 'still more / not done yet'

usage_rules: まだ + positive = 'still' — situation continues past the expected or hoped endpoint
まだまだ = emphatic doubled form — 'still have a long way to go' or 'still much left'
まだいい/まだまし = 'slightly better (than the alternative)' — resigned comparison
Time: まだ三才 = 'only three years old' (still young, early in life)

patterns.japanese_structure: まだ + [negative predicate] — not yet (hasn't reached reference point)
patterns.english_pattern: not [verb] yet / haven't [verb]ed yet / not [adj] yet

examples.jp: まだ食べていない
examples.natural: I haven't eaten yet.
examples.note: まだ + negative = not yet

examples.jp: まだ有名じゃない
examples.natural: Not famous yet.
examples.note: na-adj + negative

examples.jp: まだ！
examples.natural: Not yet!
examples.note: standalone まだ with ない omitted

usage_rules: まだ + negative = 'not yet' — the expected/hoped state hasn't arrived
まだ食べない ≠ まだ食べていない — まだ食べない = 'I won't eat yet' (refusal/choice), not 'I haven't eaten yet'
Standalone まだ！ = 'Not yet!' — response implying the situation is still in progress
DISTINGUISH: まだ (not yet) vs もう (already/anymore) — critical for reader comprehension

patterns.japanese_structure: まだ vs もう — opposite temporal perspective on same situation
patterns.english_pattern: still [positive framing] vs already/only [pessimistic framing]

examples.jp: まだ十五分もある（楽観）
examples.natural: There are still fifteen minutes left. (optimistic)
examples.note: まだ = half-full

examples.jp: もう十五分しかない（悲観）
examples.natural: There's only fifteen minutes left already. (pessimistic)
examples.note: もう + しか = half-empty

examples.jp: もう終わった
examples.natural: It's already over. / It's done already.
examples.note: もう positive = already finished

usage_rules: まだ = situation persists, reference point not yet passed (optimist's lens)
もう = reference point has been passed; already/no longer (can be positive 'already done' or lamenting 'already gone')
もうすぐ = 'soon / almost / any moment now' — about to cross the reference point
もう〜ない = 'no longer / not anymore'; まだ〜ない = 'not yet'

nara_conditional.description: なら is a conditional particle meaning 'if (given that/assuming)'. Unlike たら/ば, なら is based on assumption or received information — the condition may or may not be true. Critical for LN counters, suggestions, and counterfactuals. (Source: Tofugu — なら)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Verb/adj/noun plain form] + なら — conditional based on assumption
patterns.english_pattern: if [you're going to X] / if [it's the case that X] / should you [X]

examples.jp: 外に行くなら、ゴミ捨てて来て
examples.natural: If you're going out, take out the garbage.
examples.note: assumes listener has a plan to go out

examples.jp: コウイチに会うなら、メールを見るよう伝えてください
examples.natural: If you happen to see Koichi, let him know to check his email.
examples.note: speaker assumes listener might meet Koichi

examples.jp: 寒いならこれを着てください
examples.natural: If you're cold, please put this on.
examples.note: based on observed/assumed state

usage_rules: なら = 'given that you're [doing X], [advice/request follows]' — based on speaker's assumption
Cannot be used for certainties: ❌ 春になるなら (spring definitely comes; use 春になったら)
Cannot describe your OWN known intents: ❌ 私が行くなら〜 (you know if you're going)
Listener's uncertain plans: ⭕ 外に行くなら (since speaker doesn't know for sure)
EN: 'if you're going to...' / 'should you...' / 'in case you...' depending on register

patterns.japanese_structure: [Counterfactual condition] + なら/だったなら — if I were [X] / if it were [X]
patterns.english_pattern: if I were [X] / if it were [X] / I wish [X] were the case

examples.jp: 私があなたならそんなことはしない
examples.natural: If I were you, I wouldn't do something like that.
examples.note: classic counterfactual

examples.jp: 今日が晴れなら、キャンプへ行けたのに
examples.natural: If it were sunny today, we could have gone camping.
examples.note: present counterfactual with のに regret

examples.jp: 今日が金曜日ならなあ
examples.natural: I wish today were Friday. / If only it were Friday...
examples.note: なら at sentence end = wish/regret

usage_rules: なら for counterfactuals: condition is contrary to current reality
Past form だったなら = more remote/literary/poetic: '私が鳥だったなら…'
なら at sentence end (trailing) = expresses wish or regret: 'If only... / I wish...'
With のに: 〜なら〜のに = 'If X were true, [Y] but [Y is not happening] — regret marker'

patterns.japanese_structure: [Noun/topic] + なら — 'if it's the [topic] we're talking about'
patterns.english_pattern: as for [X] / when it comes to [X] / if it's [X] you're asking about / [X]? That I can do.

examples.jp: その帽子なら、さっき誰かが持って行きましたよ
examples.natural: If it's that hat you mean, someone took it just now.
examples.note: refers to specific topic mentioned

examples.jp: マミなら大丈夫でしょ
examples.natural: If it's Mami, she'll be fine. / Mami? She can handle it.
examples.note: based on what speaker knows about the person

examples.jp: うーん、土曜日ならいいよ
examples.natural: Well, if it's Saturday, I'm in. / Saturday works for me.
examples.note: suggesting alternate that DOES work

usage_rules: Topic-referencing なら = 'given that specific thing/person/time — then [my response]'
Often used to accept an alternative after refusing: '金曜日は無理だけど土曜日ならいいよ'
Character assessment: [name]なら = 'knowing [name], she would...' — confident prediction based on character knowledge

noni_conjunction.description: のに is a conjunctive particle connecting two clauses where the second contradicts expectation set by the first. Carries frustration, surprise, or regret. Also used in counterfactual wishes ('if only') and trailing-off lament. (Source: Tofugu — のに)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Clause A] + のに + [Clause B (contradicts expectation)] — frustration/complaint
patterns.english_pattern: even though / but / despite / and yet — with emotional weight of surprise or frustration

examples.jp: クーラーをつけたのに、全然涼しくならなかった
examples.natural: I turned on the AC, but it's still not cooling down at all.
examples.note: AC should cool → didn't = frustration

examples.jp: あんなに勉強したのに、不合格だった
examples.natural: Even though I studied that hard, I failed.
examples.note: effort → should pass → didn't = disappointment

examples.jp: せっかく掃除したのに、すぐまた汚くなった
examples.natural: I went to the trouble of cleaning, but it's already messy again.
examples.note: せっかく amplifies frustration

usage_rules: のに always implies: Clause A sets an expectation that Clause B violates
Unlike けど (neutral contrast), のに always carries emotional weight: surprise, frustration, disappointment
Nouns/な-adj before のに: insert な → なのに (レモンなのに甘い / 静かなのに眠れない)
せっかく〜のに = 'despite going to the trouble of...' — amplifies regret over wasted effort
EN: 'even though / and yet / but to no avail / for nothing' — let the frustration come through

patterns.japanese_structure: [Surprising condition] + のに + [unexpected fact]
patterns.english_pattern: even though [X], [surprising Y] / [X], and yet [Y]

examples.jp: キョーコちゃんはまだ五才なのに三ヶ国語も喋れる
examples.natural: Even though Kyoko-chan is only five, she can speak three languages.
examples.note: 5 years old → can't speak multiple languages (assumption) → exceeded

examples.jp: 夜中の三時なのに、こんなに人がいる
examples.natural: It's three in the morning, and yet there are this many people.
examples.note: 3am → few people (assumption) → not the case

usage_rules: Surprise variant: Clause A = surprising baseline; Clause B = exceeds or bucks expectation positively
EN: 'even though [surprisingly X], [Y]' — choose 'even though' vs 'and yet' based on flow

patterns.japanese_structure: [Counterfactual + result] + のに — trailing lament / counterfactual wish
patterns.english_pattern: if only [X], [Y would be different] ... / [X] but... (implying unspoken regret)

examples.jp: 電子レンジがあったら楽なのに
examples.natural: If we had a microwave, it would be so much easier... (but we don't)
examples.note: hidden reality: no microwave

examples.jp: あんなに勉強したのに…
examples.natural: I studied so hard... (trailing off, implying failure)
examples.note: sentence-final のに = lament, listener infers the rest

examples.jp: 夜中の三時なのに…
examples.natural: It's three in the morning though... (implying something unspoken)
examples.note: trailing のに invites inference

usage_rules: Trailing のに… = speaker leaves the complaint/regret unsaid — listener fills in the emotion
In LN: render with '...' or em-dash to preserve the trailing-off quality
Counterfactual のに: [したら〜のに] = 'if [condition], [result] — but that's not reality'
Often paired with のに at the end of chapter scenes for emotional resonance — preserve it in EN

particle_mo_additive.description: も is the additive particle — marks a word as a member of a group (also/too/either). Can express surprising emphasis with quantity words, or absolute meaning with question words (いつも=always, 何も=anything/nothing). (Source: Tofugu — も)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Noun] + も — 'also / too / either' additive
patterns.english_pattern: also / too / either (negative) / as well

examples.jp: 鈴木さんも日本語の先生です
examples.natural: Suzuki-san is also a Japanese teacher.
examples.note: adds Suzuki to the 'Japanese teacher' set

examples.jp: これも変じゃない
examples.natural: This isn't weird either.
examples.note: negative + も = 'either'

examples.jp: 森さんも山田さんも学生じゃありません
examples.natural: Neither Mori-san nor Yamada-san are students.
examples.note: multiple も in negative = 'neither...nor'

examples.jp: 私も！
examples.natural: Me too! / Same here!
examples.note: standalone も

usage_rules: も attaches to the specific item being added — not sentence-final like 'too' in EN
Negative sentences: も = 'either' single; 〜も〜も + negative = 'neither...nor'
Multiple positive: 森さんも山田さんも = 'both Mori-san and Yamada-san'
Replaces は/が/を/に etc. — the particle が/を disappears when も is added
でも after nouns = 'even [noun]' or 'any/no matter what kind'

patterns.japanese_structure: [Quantity/amount] + も — emphatic surprising quantity
patterns.english_pattern: as many as [N] / a whole [N] / [N] (with surprise/amazement in tone)

examples.jp: ハンバーガーを６個も食べちゃった
examples.natural: I ate a whole six hamburgers!
examples.note: も = surprising amount

examples.jp: ３時間も待った
examples.natural: I waited for THREE hours! / I waited a whole three hours.
examples.note: emphasizes that three hours is a long time

examples.jp: ５キロも太った
examples.natural: I gained five kilos! (can you believe it)
examples.note: subjective excess

usage_rules: Quantity + も = subjective emphasis — speaker finds the number surprisingly large (or small)
In EN: exclamation tone, 'a whole / as many as / no less than / a full [N]'
Context determines positive (wow, so much!) vs negative (can you believe how much!) — but も always = emphatic
Do NOT drop も as a plain number — it carries the emotional register

patterns.japanese_structure: Question word + も — universal positive (all/every) or universal negative (nothing/never)
patterns.english_pattern: always / everything / everyone / everywhere / nothing / never / anyone / anywhere

examples.jp: いつも = always
examples.natural: always
examples.note: いつ (when) + も = every (when) = always

examples.jp: 何も食べたくない
examples.natural: I don't want to eat anything.
examples.note: 何 + も + negative = nothing/anything

examples.jp: どれもおいしそう！
examples.natural: Everything looks delicious!
examples.note: どれ (which) + も = every which = all/everything

usage_rules: Question word + も + POSITIVE = 'every': いつも=always, 誰もが=everyone, どこも=everywhere
Question word + も + NEGATIVE = 'none': 何もない=nothing, 誰もいない=nobody, どこもない=nowhere
Question word + でも = 'no matter ~': いつでも=anytime, 何でも=anything, 誰でも=anyone

shi_reason_listing.description: 〜し links related items or reasons in a cumulative way — 'and what's more / and on top of that'. Implies additional unspoken reasons may exist. Softer causation than から/ので. Common in LN dialogue for excuses, praise, and character assessment. (Source: Tofugu — 〜し)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Item A] + し, [Item B] + し, [Item C] — additive parallel list
patterns.english_pattern: [Item A] and [Item B] / [Item A], not to mention [Item B] / [Item A], and what's more [Item B]

examples.jp: 花子は優しいし、かしこい
examples.natural: Hanako is kind AND smart.
examples.note: parallel positive qualities

examples.jp: 私の彼氏は有名だし、モデルだし、かっこいい
examples.natural: My boyfriend is famous, he's a model, and he's good-looking.
examples.note: noun/な-adj + だし

examples.jp: アイツはお金もないし、才能もない
examples.natural: He has no money and no talent either.
examples.note: も + し = emphatic double negation

usage_rules: い-adj: direct + し; な-adj/noun: stem + だ + し
Past: [い-adj]かったし; [noun/な-adj]だったし; verb: plain past + し
Trailing し list (unfinished): 花子は優しいし、かしこいし、かわいらしいし… = implying list goes on
し often corresponds to が/も within each listed item for emphasis: デザインも可愛いし、値段も安い

patterns.japanese_structure: [Reason/context] + し, [Suggestion/conclusion] — giving soft reasons
patterns.english_pattern: [Reason], so [suggestion] / Since [reason], why don't we... / It's [reason], so...

examples.jp: 天気もいいし、ピクニックに行かない？
examples.natural: The weather is nice, so why don't we go for a picnic?
examples.note: reason (nice weather) → gentle suggestion

examples.jp: 雨が降るみたいだし、違う日にしない？
examples.natural: It looks like rain, so how about we do it another day?
examples.note: し implies more unspoken reasons behind the rejection

examples.jp: もうすぐ二十歳になるんだし、もう少ししっかりしたら？
examples.natural: Now that you're almost twenty, maybe it's time to get your act together?
examples.note: んだし = explanatory context + soft advice

usage_rules: し as reason: softer than から/ので — implies speaker is giving a sample reason, not the only one
Perfect for rejections/declines in LN dialogue: 今日はちょっと忙しいし → 'I'm a little busy today, so...'
〜んだし = ndeshi = more explanatory/advisory nuance: 'given that [situation], why not...'
〜し、〜しで + negative situation: 'what with X and Y, it's been [bad]' — cumulative misery listing

nagara_simultaneous_contrast.description: 〜ながら has two uses: (1) simultaneous action — 'while [verb]ing, [main action]'; (2) contrasting concession — 'although [condition], [contradiction]'. The background clause always takes ながら. Restriction: simultaneous ながら requires same subject. (Source: Tofugu — 〜ながら)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Verb masu-stem] + ながら + [main action] — two actions at once (same subject)
patterns.english_pattern: while [verb]ing / [main action], [verb]ing in the background

examples.jp: 食べながら、友達とゲームで遊んだ
examples.natural: While eating, I played games with my friend.
examples.note: ながら on background action (eating)

examples.jp: 今度、お昼を食べながら話しましょう
examples.natural: Let's talk over lunch.
examples.note: eating = background; talking = main

examples.jp: コウイチは働きながら勉強している
examples.natural: Koichi studies while working. / Koichi works and goes to school.
examples.note: ongoing simultaneous states

usage_rules: ながら attaches to masu-stem of the BACKGROUND / less-important action
SAME SUBJECT ONLY — ながら cannot connect actions of different people (use 〜間に for different subjects)
LN: 'walked along, staring at her phone' — translate ながら as participial phrase or 'while [verb]ing'
Ongoing states: 働きながら勉強している = 'studying while working' (habitual concurrent situations)

patterns.japanese_structure: Noun/adj/verb-stem + ながら + [contradictory result] — 'although / even though'
patterns.english_pattern: although / even though / while / despite being [X], [Y contradicts expectation]

examples.jp: 彼は初心者ながらよくやった
examples.natural: Though he was a beginner, he did a great job.
examples.note: beginner → not expected to do well → did well

examples.jp: 知りながら、黙っていた
examples.natural: Even though I knew, I kept quiet.
examples.note: knowing + not speaking = contrast

examples.jp: 残念ながら、負けてしまいました
examples.natural: Unfortunately, I lost. / Regrettably, we lost.
examples.note: 残念ながら = fixed formal expression: 'unfortunately/regrettably'

examples.jp: 誠に勝手ながら
examples.natural: I realize this is presumptuous, but... / While I know this is asking a lot...
examples.note: formal humble opener for announcements

usage_rules: Contrast ながら: [noun] + ながら (初心者ながら); [い-adj] + ながら (小さいながら); [verb stem] + ながら (言いながら)
Expresses: my condition/action contrasts with / contradicts the expected outcome
残念ながら = fixed expression: 'unfortunately / regrettably' — very common in polite writing and announcements
誠に勝手ながら = 'acknowledging the imposition while proceeding anyway' — formal business/announcements

tarisuru_incomplete_list.description: 〜たりする lists actions, states, or scenarios as an INCOMPLETE sample — implying 'among other things / sometimes X and sometimes Y and so on'. Unlike て-form (complete sequence) or や (nouns only), たり works with verbs, adj, and nouns, and always implies the list could continue. (Source: Tofugu — 〜たりする)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Verb past] + り + [Verb past] + りする — sampling incomplete action list
patterns.english_pattern: things like [verb]ing and [verb]ing / [verb] and [verb], among other things

examples.jp: 公園で自転車に乗ったりバレーボールしたりした
examples.natural: At the park, we did things like bike-riding and volleyball.
examples.note: 〜たりした = past; implies other activities too

examples.jp: 本を読んだり、音楽を聴いたりする
examples.natural: I do things like reading books and listening to music.
examples.note: general leisure activities as sample

examples.jp: 泣いたり笑ったりする
examples.natural: laughing and crying / alternating between tears and laughter
examples.note: emotional swings — contrast pair

usage_rules: Formation: plain past form (た/だ) + り — 食べた→食べたり, した→したり
Tense is set by する at the end: したりした = past; したりする = present/general
The past-tense appearance is STRUCTURAL ONLY — does NOT mean past time by itself
vs て-form: 食べて遊んだ = did those two specific things; 食べたり遊んだりした = did those and more
EN: 'things like...', 'doing X and Y and such', 'X-ing and Y-ing and so on'

patterns.japanese_structure: [い-adj past / noun/な-adj past] + り — listing varying scenarios/states
patterns.english_pattern: sometimes [adj/state A] and sometimes [adj/state B] / can be [A] or [B]

examples.jp: この辺は暑かったり寒かったりします
examples.natural: This area is sometimes hot and sometimes cold.
examples.note: varying temperature scenarios

examples.jp: お昼ごはんはサラダだったりおにぎりだったりする
examples.natural: Sometimes my lunch is salad, other times it's onigiri.
examples.note: different scenarios on different days

examples.jp: その図書館は静かだったりうるさかったりします
examples.natural: That library can be quiet or noisy — it varies.
examples.note: mixed な-adj and い-adj

usage_rules: い-adj: drop い + かった + り (暑い→暑かったり)
な-adj/noun: stem + だったり (有名→有名だったり, 猫→猫だったり)
DIFFERENT from や (samples in ONE scenario) — たり = DIFFERENT scenarios on different occasions
Can combine: サラダやおにぎりだったりする = 'sometimes salad-and-onigiri (etc)' — ya samples within one scenario, たり marks it as one of multiple possible scenarios

tearu_intentional_state.description: 〜てある describes a CURRENT STATE that exists because SOMEONE intentionally caused it. Always uses transitive verbs. Implies purpose/initiative behind the state — 'someone set it up / left it that way / prepared it'. Contrasts with 〜ている (merely describes state) and 〜てくれる (expresses gratitude). (Source: Tofugu — 〜てある)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Transitive verb て form] + ある — state resulting from intentional past action
patterns.english_pattern: has been [past participle] / is [past participle] (someone set it up) / ready: [state]

examples.jp: メモがデスクに置いてある
examples.natural: A memo is on the desk. (someone placed it there)
examples.note: が = notice: someone left it intentionally

examples.jp: もうクリスマスプレゼントは買ってある
examples.natural: I've already bought Christmas presents.
examples.note: initiative/proactivity: already taken care of

examples.jp: 真夏なのに、暖房がつけてある
examples.natural: It's midsummer but the heater has been left on.
examples.note: unusual state — someone did this on purpose, raising question why

usage_rules: ONLY transitive verbs — the verb must describe an intentional action that leaves a changed state
ドアが開けてある (transitive 開ける) ⭕ — vs ドアが開いている (intransitive 開く) = no agent implied
Particle nuance: が = noticing; は = known topic; を = speaker's own initiative
Gratitude: if you know WHO did it and want to express thanks, 〜てくれた is more natural
EN: when emphasizing preparedness, use 'already...', 'has been...', 'ready and [past participle]'

patterns.japanese_structure: 〜てある vs 〜ている — intentional agent vs neutral state
japanese_indicators: 開けてある vs 開いている
置いてある vs 置いている
つけてある vs ついている
patterns.english_pattern: 〜てある = 'has been [verb]ed (on purpose)'; 〜ている = 'is [adj/state] (no agent context)'

examples.jp: ドアが開けてある
examples.natural: The door has been left open. (someone intentionally left it)
examples.note: transitive 開ける + てある = agent implied

examples.jp: ドアが開いている
examples.natural: The door is open.
examples.note: intransitive 開く + ている = no agent; just describing state

examples.jp: テレビがつけてある
examples.natural: Someone has turned the TV on / The TV has been left on.
examples.note: intentional vs ついている = 'the TV is on' (neutral)

usage_rules: KEY DISTINCTION: てある = someone CHOSE to create/maintain this state; ている = state exists (origin irrelevant)
In LN: てある signals that a scene has been 'set up' — someone prepared the environment; adds agency/suspense
Use てある when the identity/intent of the agent is relevant to the scene: 'Who set this up?'
Use ている when just describing how things are, without implicating anyone

patterns.japanese_structure: もう/既に + [Verb] + てある — already prepared / already taken care of
patterns.english_pattern: already [past participle] / taken care of already / [verb]ed ahead of time

examples.jp: 場所は既においてあります
examples.natural: The venue has already been reserved.
examples.note: initiative: someone already handled it

examples.jp: あ、もうコピーしてある
examples.natural: Oh, it's already been printed out.
examples.note: impressed by proactivity

examples.jp: ちゃんとメモしてあるよ
examples.natural: I've made sure to write it down.
examples.note: ちゃんと = properly/carefully + initiative

usage_rules: もう/既に + てある = 'already handled / prepared in advance' — expresses initiative or proactive preparation
In LN: often used by composed characters to signal that they've anticipated the situation
EN: 'already [pp]', 'has been [pp] already', '[pp] in advance', 'taken care of' — choose based on register

toki_time_clause.description: 〜とき creates time clauses meaning 'when [X happened/happens]'. The tense of the とき clause reflects the speaker's PERSPECTIVE (viewpoint), not objective timeline — present-tense = inside the moment; past-tense = looking back. This is the primary source of とき mistranslation. (Source: Tofugu — とき)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Verb/adj plain form] + とき — 'when [condition], [result]'
patterns.english_pattern: when [X] / at the time of [X] / whenever [X]

examples.jp: 運転をするとき、メガネをかける
examples.natural: When I drive, I wear glasses.
examples.note: habitual concurrent condition

examples.jp: 小さいとき、アメリカに住んでいた
examples.natural: When I was little, I lived in the States.
examples.note: noun + の + とき: 子供のとき = 'when I was a child'

examples.jp: 暑いとき、アイスを食べる
examples.natural: When it's hot, I eat ice cream.
examples.note: い-adj + とき directly

usage_rules: い-adj: directly + とき (暑いとき, 難しいとき)
な-adj: stem + な + とき (不安なとき, 好きなとき)
Noun: noun + の + とき (子供のとき, 雨のとき) — or noun + だったとき for contrast
と particle combinations: ときに (pinpoint moment), ときは (topic/contrast), ときも ('also when'), ときから/まで

patterns.japanese_structure: [Past-tense verb] + とき vs [Present-tense verb] + とき — perspective-based tense
japanese_indicators: 家を出たとき vs 家を出るとき
patterns.english_pattern: Both often render as 'when [past]' in EN — but tense carries nuance of viewpoint

examples.jp: 家を出たとき、カギ閉めたよね？(past — looking back from now)
examples.natural: When I left the house, I locked the door, right?
examples.note: past-tense とき = looking back from present viewpoint

examples.jp: 家を出るとき、カギ閉めたよね？(present — reliving the moment)
examples.natural: When I was leaving, I locked the door, right?
examples.note: present-tense とき = speaking from within the past moment

examples.jp: 日本に着いたとき、すぐにWi-Fi使えるかな？(future + past tense)
examples.natural: When I arrive in Japan, I wonder if I'll be able to use the Wi-Fi right away.
examples.note: future event + past-tense とき = imagining you've ALREADY arrived; focus on the moment after

usage_rules: CRITICAL: tense in とき clause = speaker's PERSPECTIVE, not objective timeline
[Past verb]とき = speaker views the event from outside/after it — 'looking back': 出たとき = after leaving
[Present verb]とき = speaker is inside the moment — 'as it's happening': 出るとき = as you're leaving
Future event + past-tense とき: 日本に着いたとき = 'once you've arrived' / 'after arriving' (focus on the resulting state)
Future event + present-tense: 日本に着くとき = 'as you're about to land' / 'as you arrive' (focus on the act)
In LN: this tense distinction creates subtle flashback texture — preserve by using 'once' vs 'as/while'

patterns.japanese_structure: とき + [various particles] — nuance modifiers
patterns.english_pattern: particle changes add emphasis/contrast/duration — mostly absorbed in EN word choice

examples.jp: この忙しいときに！
examples.natural: At a time like this?! / Of all times!
examples.note: に is essential here — cannot drop

examples.jp: 小さいときは、日本に住んでいました
examples.natural: When I was little, I lived in Japan. (as opposed to now)
examples.note: は = contrast with current situation

examples.jp: 五才のときから、日本に住んでいます
examples.natural: I've lived in Japan since I was five.
examples.note: から = starting point of duration

usage_rules: ときに = pinpoints the specific moment — cannot always drop に (この忙しいときに!! — に essential)
ときは = topic marker: contrasts 'that time' with other times or current situation
ときから = since [that time]; ときまで = until [that time]
ときが + adjective: このドアを開けるときが怖い = 'the moment of opening this door is scary' (が marks 'how I feel about this time')

toiu_versatile.description: 〜という is a phrase combining the quotation particle と and the verb 言う. It has 6 major translation-relevant functions in LN writing: ① naming ('a skill called X' / 'a person named X'), ② describing nouns with hearsay/report framing ('an article that says...'), ③ ということ for abstract facts/inference chains ('does that mean...'), ④ というより = 'rather than / more like' (character self-correction), ⑤ というか = mid-thought rephrase ('or rather... / I should say...'), ⑥ N-という-N emphasis = 'every last [noun] / anything that could be called [noun]'. Without guidance AI produces literal 'saying that...' constructions for all 6 uses. (Source: Tofugu Grammar — 〜という)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Name/term] + という/という + [noun] — 'a [noun] called [name]'
patterns.english_pattern: called [X] / named [X] / known as [X] / a [noun] by the name of [X]

examples.jp: パンジーという花
examples.natural_en: a flower called a pansy

examples.jp: 山田といいます
examples.natural_en: My name is Yamada / I'm called Yamada

examples.jp: 「猫派」とは犬より猫が好きという意味です
examples.natural_en: 'Nekoha' means you like cats more than dogs

patterns.avoid: Avoid 'a flower that is saying pansy' or 'I am saying I am Yamada'
patterns.notes: Critical for LN worldbuilding terms, skill names, class names, and character introductions. という is formal; っていう is casual version.

patterns.japanese_structure: [Clause] + という/ということ — hearsay, reported speech, or content marking
patterns.english_pattern: that [clause] / I heard that [clause] / according to [source], [clause] / the fact that [clause]

examples.jp: タカシは弁護士をしているという
examples.natural_en: I heard that Takashi is working as a lawyer

examples.jp: 店員さんによると食べられるということです
examples.natural_en: According to the staff, these are edible

examples.jp: ということは、食べられるということですか
examples.natural_en: Does that mean these are edible? / So that means...

patterns.avoid: Avoid 'it says that Takashi says he works as a lawyer'
patterns.notes: ということは at sentence start = 'Does that mean / So that means' (scene-opening inference). という alone at end of sentence = literary/formal hearsay.

patterns.japanese_structure: 〜というより — correction/contrast: 'rather than [X], more like [Y]'
patterns.english_pattern: rather than [X] / more [Y] than [X] / less [X], more [Y] / it's not so much [X] as it is [Y]

examples.jp: ピリピリするというより燃えている感じ
examples.natural_en: It's burning rather than tingling

examples.jp: 弁護士というより政治家っぽい
examples.natural_en: He's more of a politician than a lawyer

patterns.avoid: Avoid 'more than saying tingling, burning feeling'
patterns.notes: Key character voice tool for self-correction and nuanced self-description in inner monologue.

patterns.japanese_structure: N-という-N — extreme emphasis: 'every last [noun] / anything that could be called [noun]'
patterns.english_pattern: every last [noun] / all the [nouns] / anything and everything that could be called [noun]

examples.jp: 木という木が切り倒されていた
examples.natural_en: Every last tree had been cut down

examples.jp: 花という花が咲き誇っていた
examples.natural_en: Every flower was in full bloom

patterns.avoid: Avoid 'trees that say they are trees'
patterns.notes: Literary construction most common in narrative description. Particularly useful for post-battle scene devastation or spring/beauty descriptions in LN.

passive_adversative_rareru.description: 〜られる creates the Japanese passive, which has TWO types with completely different English translations: ① Direct passive (same as English) — 'I was kissed by Picasso' → done directly to the experiencer. ② Indirect/adversative passive — 'I had my wallet stolen' / 'I was rained on' — the action was done to something else but AFFECTED the experiencer against their will. The indirect passive has NO exact English equivalent and is the primary source of passive mistranslation. Doer marked by に (persons/things), から (persons only, direct), によって (formal writing). (Source: Tofugu Grammar — 〜られる Passive)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Experiencer] は [Doer] に [Verb-られる] — direct passive: action done directly to experiencer
patterns.english_pattern: [experiencer] was [verb]ed (by [doer]) — standard English passive

examples.jp: 私は蜂に刺された
examples.natural_en: I was stung by a bee

examples.jp: ピカソにキスされた
examples.natural_en: I was kissed by Picasso

examples.jp: いきなり殴られた
examples.natural_en: I was suddenly punched

patterns.avoid: OK to render as standard English passive
patterns.notes: Direct passive = standard translation. Focus special attention on indirect/adversative instances.

patterns.japanese_structure: [Affected person] は [Doer] に [Object-を] [Verb-られる] — indirect/adversative passive: done-to nuance
patterns.english_pattern: have/had [object] [verb]ed (by [doer]) / get [verb]ed on / suffer [doer]'s [action]

examples.jp: 泥棒に携帯をとられた
examples.natural_en: I had my phone stolen by a thief

examples.jp: タバコを吸われた
examples.natural_en: I was smoked on (the smoke got to me)

examples.jp: 突然雨に降られた
examples.natural_en: I got caught in the rain / I was rained on

examples.jp: 彼女に泣かれた
examples.natural_en: She cried on me (and I had to deal with it)

patterns.avoid: NEVER render as 'my phone was stolen by a thief' (loses the experiencer-impact nuance) — the point is the EFFECT ON the subject, not description of the theft
patterns.notes: Adversative passive = highest LN translation risk area. Creates the 'done TO me' victimhood/powerlessness POV essential to isekai, fantasy, trauma scenes, and relationship breakdowns. Distinguish: direct (私は彼に殴られた = I was hit by him) vs adversative (彼に財布を盗まれた = I had my wallet stolen by him).

patterns.japanese_structure: [Actor] は [Causer] に [Verb-させられる] — causative-passive: forced to do against will
patterns.english_pattern: was made to [verb] / was forced to [verb] / had to [verb] (against one's will)

examples.jp: 毎日母に納豆を食べさせられます
examples.natural_en: I'm made to eat natto every day by my mom

examples.jp: スクワットを百回させられた
examples.natural_en: I was made to do a hundred squats

patterns.avoid: Avoid 'I was allowed to eat natto' — させられる has lost the 'allow' meaning of させる; it only means compulsion
patterns.notes: Causative-passive = double compulsion: someone caused someone else to do something against their wishes. Core power-dynamic grammar in LN — villain forces protagonist, teacher tortures student.

sasuga_inevitability.description: さすが (流石) carries a nuance of 'inevitability' — not just 'as expected' but 'inevitable given a specific attribute/reason'. Two main uses: ① Compliment — the outcome matches expectations, AND the speaker is impressed: 'Only you could have done this / I'd expect nothing less from you'. ② Contradiction — an extraordinary circumstance caused an inevitable result against normal expectations: 'Even [X] couldn't overcome [this specific reason]'. Differs from やはり (which only means 'as expected') — さすが also implies a specific causal attribute and admiration. Can attach as さすが! / さすがは / さすがの + noun / さすがに + adverb. Also used sarcastically. Pairs with だけある ('you can really tell that...'). (Source: Tofugu Grammar — さすが)

patterns.japanese_structure: さすが[は/の/に] [attribute/person] — compliment: outcome inevitable given this person's attribute
patterns.english_pattern: I'd expect nothing less (from [X]) / That's [X] for you / Only [X] could do this / Just like [X] / No wonder [X]

examples.jp: さすがは鈴木部長！
examples.natural_en: I'd expect nothing less from you, Mr. Suzuki!

examples.jp: さすがに美味しい
examples.natural_en: It's exactly as good as you'd expect / Naturally it's delicious

examples.jp: さすが！
examples.natural_en: No wonder! / I knew it! / Of course!

examples.jp: さすがの歌唱力でした
examples.natural_en: That was some impressive singing ability / Quite the singing talent

patterns.avoid: NEVER render all instances as 'as expected' — loses the admiration/inevitability nuance entirely
patterns.notes: Most frequent LN character assessment word. The compliment nuance requires emphasis on the subject's inherent quality compelling the outcome.

patterns.japanese_structure: さすがに / さすがの [X] も (〜ない) — contradiction: even [X] cannot overcome [unusual circumstance]
patterns.english_pattern: even [X] couldn't / it's inevitable that even [X] failed / despite being [X], [circumstance] meant [result]

examples.jp: A5和牛だけど、さすがに美味しくない
examples.natural_en: Even A5 wagyu couldn't taste good in this state

examples.jp: さすがに限界だ
examples.natural_en: Even I have my limits / I've inevitably hit my limit

patterns.avoid: Avoid rendering contradiction-さすが as a compliment
patterns.notes: Contradiction use is highly literary and appears often in LN turning points — the hero finally admitting a limit, or the antagonist conceding to an overwhelming force.

first_person_pronoun_register.description: Japanese first-person pronouns are character archetype signals that must be rendered through English voice texture, not collapsed into a uniform 'I'. Each carries: 私/わたし = neutral/formal (feminine in casual settings); わたくし = extremely formal/elegant (princess/butler voice); 僕/ぼく = earnest, cultured, soft-masculine (gentle protagonist archetype); 俺/おれ = assertive, rough, masculine (action/delinquent; 俺様 = arrogant/sassy); うち = casual feminine, Kansai-influenced, group-unity feel. Plural suffixes carry additional register: たち neutral; ら casual; 方 honorific; ども humble/collective. (Source: Tofugu Grammar — First-Person Pronouns)

patterns.japanese_structure: 僕 — earnest, cultured, soft-masculine first-person
patterns.english_pattern: Render as 'I' but EXPRESS through: gentle word choice, politeness, vulnerability, lack of aggression in surrounding sentences

examples.jp: それ、僕の本かな？
examples.natural_en: Is that... my book? (gentle intonation)

examples.jp: 僕は将来サッカー選手になりたい
examples.natural_en: I want to be a soccer player someday (earnest, dreamy)

patterns.avoid: Do not represent 僕-character as aggressive, rough, or dismissive
patterns.notes: Boku archetype = gentle hero, bookish character, emotional male lead. In contrast to 俺 characters, boku characters apologize readily, hesitate before acting, speak politely.

patterns.japanese_structure: 俺 — assertive, rough, masculine first-person
patterns.english_pattern: Render as 'I' but EXPRESS through: terse sentences, contractions, physical descriptions, directness, minimal apologizing

examples.jp: 俺と飯食いに行かない？
examples.natural_en: Wanna grab food with me? (casual, direct)

examples.jp: 俺様
examples.natural_en: Arrogant/macho 'I' — express through extreme self-confidence in word choice

patterns.avoid: Do not render 俺-character speeches as polite, hesitant, or overly formal
patterns.notes: Ore archetype = action hero, delinquent, tsundere male, antagonist. 俺様 appears with arrogant/proud villain archetypes. The roughness must carry through into English sentence construction.

patterns.japanese_structure: 私/わたし — formal/neutral first-person; feminine in casual contexts
patterns.english_pattern: Render as 'I' — express register through formality of surrounding word choice; わたくし = heightened formality/elegance

examples.jp: わたくし、明日は伺えないんです
examples.natural_en: I'm afraid I won't be able to make it tomorrow (formal, elegant)

patterns.avoid: Do not give わたくし-speaker casual speech patterns
patterns.notes: わたくし is butler/noble register. Common in historical fantasy LN, ojou-sama archetypes, villain elegance.

causative_saseru.description: 〜させる is the causative suffix that tells us the subject CAUSES someone else to carry out an action. Two interpretations determined by actor willingness: ① Forceful: actor does NOT want to → 'make someone do' (actor marked with を for intransitive verbs). ② Permissive: actor WANTS to → 'let/allow someone do' (actor marked with に or を for intransitive). For transitive causatives, actor is always marked with に. Key constructions: させてあげる (I'll let you...), させてくれる (they let me...), させてもらう (I'm allowed to..., humble), させていただく (formal permission), させておく (allow to continue). Causative-passive させられる = forced to do — loses 'allow' meaning entirely. (Source: Tofugu Grammar — 〜させる Causative)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Causer] は [Actor-を] [Verb-させる] — forceful causative: make someone do (unwilling actor)
patterns.english_pattern: made [actor] [verb] / forced [actor] to [verb] / had [actor] [verb] against their will

examples.jp: お母さんは弟を学校に行かせた
examples.natural_en: My mom made my little brother go to school

examples.jp: 私を怒らせたな！
examples.natural_en: You made me angry!

patterns.avoid: Avoid 'let' when context implies unwillingness
patterns.notes: Power dynamic marker. Villain-over-protagonist, authority-over-subordinate, trauma cause-and-effect. The を-marking of actor is the key signal for forceful reading.

patterns.japanese_structure: [Causer] は [Actor-に] [Verb-させる] — permissive causative: let/allow someone do (willing actor)
patterns.english_pattern: let [actor] [verb] / allowed [actor] to [verb] / gave [actor] permission to [verb]

examples.jp: コウイチはマミにベーコンを好きなだけ食べさせた
examples.natural_en: Koichi let Mami eat as much bacon as she liked

examples.jp: 両親は私を大学に行かせてくれた
examples.natural_en: My parents let me go to university / My parents allowed me to go to university

examples.jp: もう少し考えさせてください
examples.natural_en: Please let me think about this a little longer

patterns.avoid: Avoid 'made' when actor is willing
patterns.notes: Permission constructions are key for confessions, parent-child negotiations, hero asking mentor for permission. させてもらいます = highly deferential 'I'll be taking the liberty of...' (formal Japanese workplace speech).

temiru_exploratory.description: 〜てみる combines the て form with the auxiliary verb みる (to see/look). The core meaning is 'do the action and SEE what happens' — an exploratory attempt where the goal is discovery. Key constraint: 〜てみる implies the action was COMPLETED (you can't use it for failed attempts). Usage: 食べてみる = 'try eating (to see what it's like)' not just 'eat'. Extensions: 〜てみたい = want to try...; 〜てみよう = let's try...; 〜てみたら = when I tried X, I discovered Y (triggering discovery); 〜てみたら？ = why don't you try...?; 〜てもみない = didn't even try. (Source: Tofugu Grammar — 〜てみる)

patterns.japanese_structure: [V-て] + みる — try [verb]ing to see what happens / exploratory attempt
patterns.english_pattern: try [verb]ing / [verb] and see / give [X] a try / [verb] just to see what it was like

examples.jp: 食べてみるよ
examples.natural_en: I'll try it / I'll give it a taste

examples.jp: 開けてみよう
examples.natural_en: Let's open it and see / Let's find out what's inside

examples.jp: 飲んでみたら、結構美味しかった
examples.natural_en: When I tried it, it turned out to be pretty good

patterns.avoid: Do not translate as just 'ate' or 'drank' — the exploratory/first-experience nuance is the meaning
patterns.notes: Signature YA protagonist voice — curiosity, willingness to act on instinct. てみたら + discovery = scene-opening insight formula very common in LN first-person narration.

patterns.japanese_structure: 〜てみたい / 〜てみよう — want to try / let's try (exploratory desire or invitation)
patterns.english_pattern: want to try [verb]ing / I'd love to [verb] and see / let's [verb] and find out

examples.jp: いつか火星に行ってみたい
examples.natural_en: I want to go to Mars someday and experience it

examples.jp: 私もやってみたい！
examples.natural_en: I want to try that too!

patterns.avoid: Avoid 'I want to go to Mars' without the exploratory nuance for てみたい
patterns.notes: Character ambition language. The addition of みたい signals the protagonist hasn't done this yet and craves the discovery aspect — heightens reader investment.

teoku_preparation.description: 〜ておく combines the て form with おく (to put/place). Core meaning: get the action DONE so you can put it out of your mind — proactive completion for future benefit. Three uses: ① In advance for future convenience: 勉強しておく = 'study now so I'm ready later'; ② Tentative/temporary solution: とりあえず頼んでおく = 'order something for now'; ③ Leave as-is: 自転車をとめておいたら = 'I left the bike there (and then...)'. Casual form: 〜とく / 〜どく. Compare with tearu (object-centered result state) vs teoku (person-centered proactive action). (Source: Tofugu Grammar — 〜ておく)

patterns.japanese_structure: [V-て] + おく — do in advance, proactively, to be prepared
patterns.english_pattern: do [X] in advance / get [X] done ahead of time / [verb] now so that [future benefit] / take care of [X] beforehand

examples.jp: 日本語を勉強しておく
examples.natural_en: I'm going to study Japanese in advance (before the trip)

examples.jp: 予約しておいてくれないかな
examples.natural_en: Could you make the reservation ahead of time (when you get a chance)?

examples.jp: 今のうちに勉強しておこう
examples.natural_en: I should get some studying done while I have the time

patterns.avoid: Do not drop the 'in advance / proactive' nuance — it changes the character's relationship to the action
patterns.notes: Proactive character initiative marker. When a protagonist uses teoku, they are planning ahead, showing strategic thinking. Essential for hero-preparation scenes, boss-battle prep, and countdown moments.

patterns.japanese_structure: [V-て] + おいた(ら) — left [something] in a state (often as precursor to consequence)
patterns.english_pattern: had left [X] / left [X] there / left it as it was — often followed by an unexpected result

examples.jp: 店の前に自転車をとめておいたら、盗まれた
examples.natural_en: I had left my bicycle in front of the shop — and it got stolen

examples.jp: 電気をつけっぱなしにしておいたら、怒られた
examples.natural_en: I left the light on and got scolded for it

patterns.avoid: Avoid 'I decided to park' — teoku-leave doesn't necessarily imply purposeful decision
patterns.notes: Common ironic plot device in LN — character sets up a situation (teoku) and then suffers unexpected consequences. Creates the 'and then...' narrative momentum.

kamoshirenai_possibility.description: 〜かもしれない expresses UNCERTAINTY — 'may / might / possibly'. It is the WEAKEST degree of confidence in the JP speculation scale: かもしれない (weakest) < と思う < だろう/でしょう (strongest). Cannot be combined with high-certainty adverbs like きっと or 必ず. Works well with もしかしたら (perhaps) and conditional forms 〜ば/〜たら. Special uses: ① Adding ambiguity to feelings: すきかも = 'I think I might be in love' (indirect Japanese emotional style); ② かもしれぬ = archaic/literary form (aged/dignified character voice); ③ かもしれん = Kansai/casual dialect. (Source: Tofugu Grammar — 〜かもしれない)

patterns.japanese_structure: [clause] + かもしれない/かも — weak possibility without solid evidence
patterns.english_pattern: might [verb] / may [be/do] / possibly [X] / it could be that [X] / there's a chance that [X]

examples.jp: 雨が降るかもしれない
examples.natural_en: It might rain

examples.jp: ピザ屋さんかもしれない
examples.natural_en: It might be a pizza delivery person

examples.jp: すきかも
examples.natural_en: I think... I might like you (deliberately indirect confession)

examples.jp: アイツは来ないかもしれぬ
examples.natural_en: He may not show up (archaic/dignified character voice)

patterns.avoid: Do not upgrade to 'probably' or 'surely' — this is specifically the weakest certainty register. Distinguish from だろう (stronger, has a reason behind it)
patterns.notes: Key inner monologue pattern. Japanese characters express emotions through かも as an indirect hedge — 'I might like you' instead of 'I like you'. The indirectness IS the emotional texture. Do not strengthen it.

darou_speculation.description: 〜だろう / 〜でしょう expresses SPECULATION with a REASON behind it — stronger than かもしれない. Core nuance: 'I believe X, because I have reason to think so' (even if unstated). Three uses: ① Forward speculation: コウイチは金持ちだろう = 'Koichi is probably rich (I have reason to think this)'; ② Seeking confirmation (assertive): 食べただろう = 'You ate it, right? (I'm pretty sure)' — often だろ in speech; ③ Wondering-aloud (self-directed): なんだろう = 'I wonder what that is...' — literary/introspective register. Can combine with adverbs: きっと = definitely; 多分 = probably (lower certainty); 恐らく = I'm afraid/presumably. でしょうか = formal literary introspection common in LN narrators. (Source: Tofugu Grammar — だろう)

patterns.japanese_structure: [clause] + だろう/でしょう — reasoned speculation/prediction
patterns.english_pattern: probably [X] / I imagine [X] / I assume [X] / [X] must be / surely [X] / it's likely that [X]

examples.jp: コウイチは金持ちだろう
examples.natural_en: Koichi is probably rich / I'd guess Koichi is rich

examples.jp: 明日は多分寒いだろう
examples.natural_en: It'll probably be cold tomorrow

examples.jp: 明日はきっと寒いだろう
examples.natural_en: It's definitely going to be cold tomorrow

patterns.avoid: Do not reduce to 'maybe' — だろう is stronger than かもしれない; avoid 'as expected' — that would be やはり
patterns.notes: Narrator speculation register. In LN third-person limited narration, だろう is used by the narrator voice to infer about other characters' states — 'he was probably thinking...'

patterns.japanese_structure: [question word] + だろう(か) — wondering aloud, introspective
patterns.english_pattern: I wonder what/who/where/when/why [X] / what could [X] be / where on earth is [X]

examples.jp: あれ、なんだろう？
examples.natural_en: I wonder what that is...

examples.jp: コウイチはどこだろう
examples.natural_en: I wonder where Koichi is

examples.jp: どうしたんだろう
examples.natural_en: I wonder what happened / what's going on, I wonder

patterns.avoid: Do not render as a direct question — this is introspective/rhetorical
patterns.notes: One of the most common LN inner monologue constructions. The protagonist 'wonders' about something they observe — key for building mystery, curiosity, and reader engagement. でしょうか in this position = highly literary/formal narrator voice.

uchi_temporal_window.description: うち derives from 内 ('inside') and when used with particles indicates 'within a bounded range'. Two main translation-relevant uses: ① Narrowing scope: 三人のうち二人 = 'two of the three / two out of three'. ② Temporal window: 〜うちに = 'while [X] still lasts / before [X] ends / while you still can'. The key literary nuance is URGENCY + FINITUDE — うち highlights the end boundary of the state, unlike 〜間に which simply specifies an interval. うち can follow nouns の, い-adjectives directly, な-adjectives + な, or verbs (especially いる/〜ている/〜ない forms). Rule: clause before うちに must be in NON-PAST tense even if main clause is past. (Source: Tofugu Grammar — うち)

patterns.japanese_structure: [non-past state/condition] + うちに — 'while [X] still lasts / before [X] ends'
patterns.english_pattern: while [X] still [lasts/holds] / before [X] ends / while I/you still can / while there's still time to [verb]

examples.jp: 寝れるうちに、寝ておこう
examples.natural_en: Let's sleep while we still can

examples.jp: 忘れないうちに、メモしておこう
examples.natural_en: Before I forget, I'll make a note of it

examples.jp: 若いうちは苦労しておいた方がいい
examples.natural_en: It's better to struggle while you're still young

examples.jp: 今年のうちに日本人の友だちをつくる！
examples.natural_en: I'll make Japanese friends before this year is over!

patterns.avoid: Do not use simple 'while' — the end-boundary urgency must be felt
patterns.notes: High literary density. '〜うちに' carries melancholy + urgency that defines emotionally resonant LN moments: 'while [person] is still here', 'while I still remember you', 'before my time runs out'. Translate the finitude.

naru_transformation.description: なる (to become) expresses change of state/transformation. Patterns: ① Noun/な-adjective + に + なる = 'become [X]'; ② い-adjective + く + なる = 'become [adj-er] / get/grow [adj]'; ③ Verb + ようになる = 'come to do / start doing / grow to [verb]'; ④ Verb + そうになる = 'was almost about to [verb] / nearly [verb]ed'; ⑤ ことになる = 'it was decided that / it turned out that' (outside intentional control). と instead of に = literary/formal, indicates arrival at ultimate stage. (Source: Tofugu Grammar — なる)

patterns.japanese_structure: Noun/な-adj + に + なる — become [X] / turn into [X]
patterns.english_pattern: become [X] / turn into [X] / grow into [X] / end up as [X]

examples.jp: モデルになる
examples.natural_en: become a model

examples.jp: 大切な人になった
examples.natural_en: She'd become someone precious to me

examples.jp: いそがしくなる
examples.natural_en: It's going to get busy / Things are going to get hectic

patterns.avoid: なる is a PROCESS verb — convey the transition, not just the end state
patterns.notes: Character arc backbone. Japanese LN character development is described through accumulation of なる verbs — strength, feeling, relationships all expressed as dynamic change.

patterns.japanese_structure: [Verb plain/potential form] + ようになる — come to do / start doing / reach a new ability
patterns.english_pattern: came to [verb] / started [verb]ing / grew to [verb] / can now [verb] (ability unlocked)

examples.jp: ブロッコリーを食べるようになった
examples.natural_en: He came to eat broccoli / He started eating broccoli

examples.jp: ブロッコリーを食べられるようになった
examples.natural_en: He became able to eat broccoli / He could finally eat broccoli

examples.jp: またブロッコリーを食べなくなった
examples.natural_en: He stopped eating broccoli again

patterns.avoid: Avoid pure state statements ('he eats broccoli now') — the CHANGE/ARRIVAL is the meaning
patterns.notes: Growth arc language. Used for all character development in LN: skill acquisition (ようになった after training), relationship shifts (可愛いと思うようになった = 'I grew to find her cute'), and threshold moments.

patterns.japanese_structure: [V-そう形] + になる — was about to / almost [verb]ed
patterns.english_pattern: almost [verb]ed / was about to [verb] / came close to [verb]ing

examples.jp: 泣きそうになった
examples.natural_en: I almost cried

examples.jp: 遅れそうになった
examples.natural_en: I was almost late

patterns.avoid: Avoid 'looked like about to cry' — the speaker describes their own near-action
patterns.notes: Emotional threshold language. Crucial for restraint/breakthrough moments in LN — the moment before a character finally cries, breaks down, or acts on impulse. Very common in confession scenes.

tara_conditional.description: 〜たら is formed by adding ら to the past tense (〜た) form. It is the MOST COMMON conditional form in LN dialogue. Three uses: ① Time sequence ('when/after'): 走ったらストレッチする = 'I stretch after running'; ② Conditional ('if'): もしこのプリン食べたら帰ります = 'If you eat this pudding, I'll leave'; ③ Discovery trigger (走っていたら友達に会った): 'when I was running, I ran into a friend' — the past-progressive version describes an unexpected discovery during ongoing action. ④ Reference/suggestion: コウイチだったら = '(If it's Koichi you're looking for)' / たら？ at end = 'why not?'. Counterfactual with のに: お金があったら留学するのに = 'If I had money, I would study abroad'. (Source: Tofugu Grammar — 〜たら)

patterns.japanese_structure: [V-たら] + [result clause (uncontrollable)] — when/after X, Y occurred
patterns.english_pattern: when [X] / after [X] / upon [X]ing / as soon as [X]

examples.jp: 走ったらストレッチをします
examples.natural_en: After running, I stretch

examples.jp: 走っていたら友達に会った
examples.natural_en: When I was running, I ran into a friend

examples.jp: 開けてみたら、猫だった
examples.natural_en: When I opened it, there was a cat inside

patterns.avoid: Avoid using 'if' when the context is time-sequence
patterns.notes: Discovery-formula: V-てみたら + discovery = character finds something unexpected while doing something. Extremely common LN scene opening device.

patterns.japanese_structure: もし + [V-たら] + [result clause] — 'if' conditional
patterns.english_pattern: if [X] (then) [Y] / if [X] were the case / should [X] happen

examples.jp: もしお金があったら留学するのに
examples.natural_en: If I had money, I'd study abroad

examples.jp: もしうまくいったら、バズるかもしれない
examples.natural_en: If it goes well, it might go viral

patterns.avoid: When のに follows, it signals regret/frustration about counterfactual — emphasize that longing
patterns.notes: たら + のに = regret construction. Common in LN emotional climaxes: 'If only I had been stronger...' / 'If only I had said something then...'

tehoshii_interpersonal.description: 〜てほしい combines the て form with ほしい (want/desire). Expresses: 'I want SOMEONE ELSE to do [X]' or 'I want [event] to happen'. Crucially different from 〜たい (want to do X myself). てほしい = emotional appeal to another person — the subject's desire is directed outward. Particles: に = pin a specific person as the desired doer; が = mark a situation you hope will happen. Past form てほしかった = 'I wish [X] had happened (but it didn't)' — regret particle. Negative forms: てほしくない (softer) vs ないでほしい (stronger request to NOT do). Question form てほしい？ (rising intonation) = 'Do you want me to...?' (can sound arrogant). させてほしい = 'Please let me [verb]' (asking for permission). (Source: Tofugu Grammar — 〜てほしい)

patterns.japanese_structure: [V-て] + ほしい — want someone else to do [verb] / want [event] to happen
patterns.english_pattern: want [someone] to [verb] / wish [someone] would [verb] / need [someone] to [verb]

examples.jp: 掃除機をかけてほしい
examples.natural_en: I want you to vacuum / I need someone to vacuum

examples.jp: もっと早く教えてほしかったよ
examples.natural_en: I wish someone had told me sooner

examples.jp: そういうことは言ってほしくない
examples.natural_en: I don't want you saying things like that

examples.jp: 留学させてほしい
examples.natural_en: Please let me study abroad / I want you to let me go

patterns.avoid: Do not render as 'I want to...' — てほしい is ALWAYS about another person doing something
patterns.notes: Emotional appeal grammar. Characters use てほしい in confessions ('I want you to stay'), pleas ('please don't leave me'), and demands ('I need you to understand'). Higher emotional intensity than simple requests.

tsumori_predetermined_intention.description: つもり expresses a PREDETERMINED INTENTION — a decision made in advance, not on the spot. If the decision is spontaneous, use 〜と思う instead. Key forms: ① Verb + つもり = 'I'm planning to [verb] / I'm committed to [verb]ing'; ② 〜ないつもり = 'I intend NOT to [verb]'; ③ 〜つもりだった = 'I had intended to [verb] but couldn't' (broken intention); ④ 〜たつもり = 'I believe I have [verb]ed / I thought I did...' (self-assessment, often with uncertainty); ⑤ そのつもり = 'That's what I mean to do / That's the plan'; ⑥ そんなつもりじゃなかった = 'I didn't mean it like that' (crucial apologetic construction). (Source: Tofugu Grammar — つもり)
