patterns.japanese_structure: [Verb plain form] + つもり — settled, pre-decided intention
patterns.english_pattern: plan to [verb] / intend to [verb] / I'm set on [verb]ing / I'm committed to [verb]ing

examples.jp: ラーメンを食べるつもりです
examples.natural_en: I'm planning to eat ramen

examples.jp: この店を売るつもりはない！
examples.natural_en: I have no intention of selling this shop!

examples.jp: いつかイラストレーターになるつもりです
examples.natural_en: I'm determined to become an illustrator someday

patterns.avoid: Do not use 'want to' — つもり is POST-decision; the character has already made up their mind
patterns.notes: Character resolve language. Used for protagonist declarations, villain announcements, and decisive moments. The firmness IS the nuance.

patterns.japanese_structure: [Verb] + つもりだった — had intended to but failed / was going to but didn't
patterns.english_pattern: was going to [verb] (but...) / had intended to [verb] / I meant to [verb] / I'd planned on [verb]ing

examples.jp: 本気で結婚するつもりだったんだよ
examples.natural_en: I really was going to marry you

examples.jp: ダイエットするつもりだったけど、チョコレートには勝てない
examples.natural_en: I was going to diet, but I can't resist chocolate

patterns.avoid: Maintain the past-intention-vs-present-reality gap — the pathos is in the broken plan
patterns.notes: One of the most emotionally resonant grammar patterns in LN. 〜つもりだった + のに = bitter regret. 〜つもりだったけど = wry self-deprecation. Both are core romance/drama scene patterns.

patterns.japanese_structure: [V-た] + つもり — 'as if I had / believing I had / I thought I did...'
patterns.english_pattern: I thought I had [verb]ed / I believed I [verb]ed / I was sure I had [verb]ed / pretending to have [verb]ed

examples.jp: 十分勉強したつもりだけど…
examples.natural_en: I thought I'd studied enough, but...

examples.jp: そんなつもりじゃなかったんです
examples.natural_en: I didn't mean it like that / That wasn't my intention

patterns.avoid: そんなつもりじゃなかった = NOT 'I didn't plan that' but 'I didn't mean/intend that impression/effect'
patterns.notes: Critical apologetic construction. Very common in misunderstanding scenes in LN romance — 'That wasn't what I meant at all.'

second_person_pronoun_register.description: Japanese second-person pronouns each encode relationship + power + intimacy dynamics. All tend to acquire directness/rudeness over time due to the nature of directly addressing someone. あなた = most neutral; condescending in close relationships when used in formal mode; 婦人 term of endearment (married woman→husband, old-fashioned but used in LN character development). 君/きみ = literary/poetic register paired with 僕; condescending in workplace; affectionate in creative writing. お前/おまえ = rough masculine; anger register OR deep casual intimacy. 貴様/きさま = historically polite but now EXTREMELY hostile/confrontational (villain speech). In Japanese, pronouns are often REPLACED by names or social roles — using a pronoun at all signals a specific relationship dynamic. (Source: Tofugu Grammar — Second-Person Pronouns)

patterns.japanese_structure: 君 — literary/poetic 'you'; condescending in power contexts
patterns.english_pattern: Render as 'you' — express literary register through: poetic surrounding phrasing, song-lyric cadence, slightly elevated diction

examples.jp: 君の名は。
examples.natural_en: Your Name. (hit anime title — literary/poetic register)

examples.jp: 君、これやっておいてくれないかな
examples.natural_en: Hey, can you take care of this for me? (condescending boss)

patterns.avoid: Distinguish between 君 as literary warmth (with 僕 pairing) vs 君 as workplace condescension
patterns.notes: Kimi/boku pairing = Meiji-era academic/cultured intimacy. Still present in LN as established male protagonist voice when addressing rival or love interest. Literary translation should preserve slight elevation.

patterns.japanese_structure: お前 — rough masculine 'you'; intense anger or deep casual intimacy
patterns.english_pattern: Render as 'you' — express through: clipped sentences, profanity register in anger context, extreme familiarity in intimacy context

examples.jp: お前、ふざけてんのか？
examples.natural_en: Are you kidding me? / What the hell is wrong with you?

examples.jp: お前が好きだ
examples.natural_en: I love you (from a rough-voiced character — directness of omae makes this rawer)

patterns.avoid: Do not render omae-speaker with polite/formal speech patterns
patterns.notes: High stakes in action/delinquent/fantasy LN. Omae between rivals = confrontational dominance display. Omae between close friends = rough intimacy. Context determines which register applies.

patterns.japanese_structure: 貴様 — highly hostile/confrontational 'you' (formerly polite, now used in combat/villainy)
patterns.english_pattern: Render as 'you' — but express through: aggressive sentence structure, confrontational punctuation, extreme hostility in word choice

examples.jp: 貴様、何者だ！
examples.natural_en: Who the hell are you?! / Just who do you think you are?!

patterns.avoid: Never render 貴様 with polite or neutral tone
patterns.notes: Villain, enemy combat, or extreme anger marker. Its etymology (noble + honorific) creates a specific literary tension — an originally respectful form weaponized into confrontation.

koto_ga_aru_experiential.description: ことがある is a grammatical pattern using こと (nominalizer) + が + ある (to exist). It has TWO distinct patterns that must NOT be conflated: ① Present + ことがある: 'it happens that / sometimes does / occasionally' — HABITUAL/OCCASIONAL. e.g. 授業をサボることがある = 'I sometimes skip class'. ② Past + たことがある: 'have done [X] before' — EXPERIENTIAL PERFECT. e.g. 日本に行ったことがある = 'I have been to Japan (at least once, as a life experience)'. Particle variation matters: が = neutral; も = 'also/can also be' (implies contrast with a primary state); は = slight topicalizing emphasis. こともあれば、こともある = 'sometimes X, sometimes Y' (alternating states). (Source: Tofugu Grammar — 〜ことがある)

patterns.japanese_structure: [Verb/adj plain present form] + ことがある/こともある — 'sometimes does / occasionally happens / can also be'
patterns.english_pattern: sometimes [verb] / occasionally [verb] / there are times when [clause] / can also be [adj] / on occasion [clause]

examples.jp: 授業をサボることがある
examples.natural_en: There are times I skip class / I sometimes skip class

examples.jp: 夜中に目が覚めることがある
examples.natural_en: I sometimes wake up in the middle of the night

examples.jp: 日本語の文法は、面白いこともある
examples.natural_en: Japanese grammar can also be interesting (implying: despite often being hard)

examples.jp: ここのラーメンは、まずいことがない
examples.natural_en: The ramen here is never bad (emphatic absolute — implies it's always wonderful)

patterns.avoid: Do not confuse with the experiential past form. With particle も: MUST convey the contrast/additive nuance ('can also be / in addition') — not just 'sometimes'. With ことがない: renders as 'never' with emphatic finality, not just 'not sometimes'.
patterns.notes: Character habit/quirk establishment. Particle choice changes nuance: が = neutral occasional fact; も = also/additionally (implies contrast, common in LN for dual-nature characters: '彼女は怖いこともある'); は = slight topical emphasis. ことがない (present) = emphatic 'never does X', character trait defined by absolute absence — different from たことがない (has never experienced X).

patterns.japanese_structure: [clause A] + こともあれば、[clause B] + こともある — 'sometimes A, sometimes B'
japanese_indicators: こともあれば〜こともある
patterns.english_pattern: sometimes [A], and other times [B] / there are times when [A], and times when [B] / [A] one moment, [B] the next

examples.jp: 彼女は優しいこともあれば、怖いこともある
examples.natural_en: Sometimes she's kind, but other times she's terrifying

examples.jp: オレから彼女に電話することもあれば、彼女からかかってくることもある
examples.natural_en: Sometimes I call her, and sometimes she calls me

examples.jp: 本当のこともあれば、ウソのこともある
examples.natural_en: Some of it is true, and some of it is false

patterns.avoid: Do not flatten to 'sometimes A and sometimes B' as a single clause — the こともあれば、こともある construction implies two sides of a coin, a dual nature or oscillating reality
patterns.notes: High literary value for LN character description. The construction is the grammar of contradictory character traits ('she can be sweet, but she can also be cruel'), relationship oscillation, and dual-nature world description. Very common in first-person narrator voice profiling antagonists or love interests.

patterns.japanese_structure: [V-た past form] + ことがある — 'have done [X] before / [X] is part of one's life experience'
patterns.english_pattern: have [verb]ed before / have experienced [X] / [X] once / know what it's like to [verb]

examples.jp: 日本に行ったことがある
examples.natural_en: I've been to Japan before / I've had the experience of going to Japan

examples.jp: こんなに怖いと思ったことがなかった
examples.natural_en: I'd never felt this afraid before

examples.jp: 彼女に裏切られたことがある
examples.natural_en: I've been betrayed by a girl before / I know what it's like to be betrayed by someone you trusted

patterns.avoid: Do not reduce to simple past — 'I went to Japan' loses the 'as life experience' emphasis
patterns.notes: Backstory grammar. In LN, たことがある sets up protagonist/character history that contextualizes present behavior. The negative (たことがない) = character has never experienced this → first-time impact is more meaningful.

advanced_patterns.description: Advanced grammar patterns for literary and sophisticated writing

subjunctive_mood.description: Subjunctive mood for hypothetical, wishes, and demands

patterns.japanese_structure: ～たらいいのに / ～ればいいのに
patterns.english_pattern: I wish X were / If only X were

examples.jp: 彼女がここにいたらいいのに
examples.literal: It would be good if she was here
examples.natural: I wish she were here / If only she were here

usage_rules: Use 'were' for all persons in formal subjunctive
'I wish' for present contrary-to-fact
'I wish' + had + past participle for past regret

inversion_structures.description: Subject-verb inversion for emphasis and formality

patterns.japanese_structure: ～にもかかわらず / ～のに
patterns.english_pattern: even though / despite / in spite of

examples.jp: 雨が降っているにもかかわらず、彼は出かけた
examples.literal: Despite the rain falling, he went out
examples.natural: Even though it was raining, he went out / He went out despite the rain

usage_rules: 'even though' + clause (subject + verb)
'despite/in spite of' + noun/gerund only
'although' is more formal than 'even though'

patterns.japanese_structure: いくら～ても / どんなに～ても
patterns.english_pattern: much as / however much / no matter how

examples.jp: どんなに頑張っても、できない
examples.literal: No matter how hard I try, I can't do it
examples.natural: However hard I try, I just can't do it / Much as I try, it's impossible

usage_rules: 'much as' is literary/formal
'no matter how' is more common
'however' + adjective/adverb (no 'very')

participial_phrases.description: Using participles for concise, sophisticated prose

patterns.japanese_structure: ～ながら / ～つつ
patterns.english_pattern: [verb+ing], [main clause] / [main clause], [verb+ing]

examples.jp: 音楽を聴きながら、勉強した
examples.literal: While listening to music, I studied
examples.natural: Listening to music, I studied / I studied, listening to music

usage_rules: Participle and main verb must share same subject
More concise than 'while' clauses
Can place at start or end for emphasis

patterns.japanese_structure: ～された / ～られた (passive result)
patterns.english_pattern: [past participle], [main clause]

examples.jp: 驚かされて、彼は言葉を失った
examples.literal: Being surprised, he lost words
examples.natural: Shocked, he was speechless / Taken aback, he couldn't speak

usage_rules: Omit 'being' when implied
Creates more vivid, immediate prose
Subject of participle = subject of main clause

nominalization_reduction.description: Avoiding excessive nominalization for clearer prose

patterns.japanese_structure: ～することができる
patterns.english_pattern: can [verb] (not 'can do the [verb+ing] of')

examples.jp: 彼は速く走ることができる
examples.literal: He can do the running quickly
examples.natural: He can run fast

usage_rules: Prefer verbs over abstract nouns
Avoid 'make/do/have' + nominalization
Exception: established phrases like 'make a decision'

patterns.japanese_structure: ～の動作 / ～という行為
patterns.english_pattern: Reduce to single verb when possible

examples.jp: 彼女の歌う行為は美しい
examples.literal: The act of her singing is beautiful
examples.natural: She sings beautifully / Her singing is beautiful

usage_rules: Reduce 'the act of [gerund]' to gerund alone
Convert noun phrases to verbs for clarity
Keep nominalization only for technical/legal writing

redundancy_reduction.description: Avoiding repetitive terms in close proximity by using pronouns or demonstratives for natural flow

patterns.japanese_structure: ～について / ～を / ～が (repeated noun phrase)
patterns.english_pattern: Use 'that', 'it', 'this', 'one' for second mention

examples.literal:
As Kazehaya had just mentioned, it looked like they had decided to invite the entire class to participate in this test of courage.

They must have thought just doing a test of courage would be boring, so they decided to collect a participation fee and even prepare prizes.
examples.natural:
As Kazehaya had just mentioned, it looked like they had decided to invite the entire class to participate in this test of courage.

They must have thought just doing that would be boring, so they decided to collect a participation fee and even prepare prizes.

usage_rules: When noun phrase appears in sentence N, use pronoun/demon...
Applies when referent is clear from immediate context
Use 'that' for abstract concepts just mentioned

patterns.japanese_structure: ～すること / ～するの (repeated action phrase)
patterns.english_pattern: Use 'doing that', 'doing so', 'that' for second mention

examples.literal:
I was nervous about going to the party.

But I decided going to the party was necessary.
examples.natural:
I was nervous about going to the party.

But I decided it was necessary.
examples.fix: going to the party → it (action already stated)

usage_rules: Replace repeated gerund phrases with pronouns
Use 'doing that' when emphasizing the action aspect
Use 'it' when treating action as a thing/state

patterns.japanese_structure: Name repetition in adjacent sentences
japanese_indicators: Character name at start of consecutive sentences
patterns.english_pattern: Use pronoun after first mention in paragraph

examples.literal:
Sawako entered the classroom nervously.

Sawako noticed everyone staring at her.
examples.natural:
Sawako entered the classroom nervously.

She noticed everyone staring at her.

usage_rules: Use pronouns for same subject in adjacent sentences
Exception: Restate name after speaker change in dialogue
Exception: Restate name at paragraph breaks

comedic_timing.description: Patterns for maximizing comedic impact through punchy, concise delivery - especially critical for romcom/ecchi comedy

patterns.japanese_structure: ～より～が嫌い / ～というより～
patterns.english_pattern: Drop connecting words, use shorter sentence for punchline

examples.jp: こいつ、本が苦手というより頭を使うことが嫌いなんだよな
examples.literal: It's not that this girl hates books; she just hates using her brain.
examples.natural: She doesn't hate books. She hates thinking.

usage_rules: Split into two short sentences for comedic beat
Second sentence is the punchline - keep it shortest
Drop softening language like 'rather', 'it's just that'

patterns.japanese_structure: どこの世界に～するヤツがいるんだ
patterns.english_pattern: Drop rhetorical framing, state directly

examples.jp: どこの世界に人の部屋にやってきて、いきなり布団を敷き始めるヤツがいるんだ
examples.literal: Who in the world walks into someone's room and immediately starts laying out their futon?
examples.natural: Who just walks into someone's room and starts unrolling their futon?

usage_rules: Remove 'in the world' for punchier delivery
Drop modifiers like 'immediately' if they dilute impact
Keep verbs active and direct

patterns.japanese_structure: オスになった～の匂い
patterns.english_pattern: Use blunt noun compounds, avoid flowery description

examples.jp: もう、オスになった駿太の匂いを楽しんでたのに
examples.literal: Aw, I was just enjoying the scent of Shunta-turned-man.
examples.natural: I was enjoying your man-smell.

usage_rules: Blunt compound nouns are funnier than descriptions
Drop possessive constructions when possible
Short = funnier for absurd situations

patterns.japanese_structure: ～なら～ない (if-then negation)
patterns.english_pattern: Reduce to colon format for deadpan delivery

examples.jp: おっぱいが見られないならこの旅館に用はない
examples.literal: Is it too heartless to think that if I can't see boobs, I have no business being at this inn?
examples.natural: Is it heartless to think: no boobs, no reason to be here?

usage_rules: Use colon to create comedic beat/pause
Reduce conditional to minimal phrase
Parallel structure amplifies humor (X, no Y)

patterns.japanese_structure: 淡々とした描写
patterns.english_pattern: State absurd situations in plain language

examples.jp: 幼なじみでも、高校生にもなったらお互いの家なんて行かないでしょ
examples.literal: Childhood friends or not, when you're in high school, you don't really go to each other's houses.
examples.natural: Childhood friends or not, high schoolers don't really go over to each other's houses, you know.

usage_rules: Avoid embellishing absurd situations
Direct statement > flowery description
Let situation be funny, not the language

patterns.japanese_structure: ～じゃないか / ～なのか
patterns.english_pattern: Drop softening, use short exclamation for reaction

examples.jp: どうしてそこで田中が出て来るんだよっ！
examples.literal: Why does Tanaka have to come up now?!
examples.natural: Why does Tanaka have to come up now?!

usage_rules: Keep exclamations short and direct
Use repeated letters for emphasis (Tanakaaa!!)
Multiple exclamation marks for comedic intensity

patterns.japanese_structure: ～わけないだろ / ～はずないし
patterns.english_pattern: Use 'There's no way' or direct negation

examples.jp: 俺が優里亜のこと臭いとか思うわけないだろ！
examples.literal: There's no way I'd ever think Yuria smells bad!
examples.natural: There's no way I'd ever think Yuria smells bad!

usage_rules: 'There's no way' for strong denial
Keep the absurd detail in the quote
Exclamation mark for emphasis

patterns.japanese_structure: Repetitive emphasis for comedy
patterns.english_pattern: Keep repetition for comedic escalation

examples.jp: あーもう！　ち●ち●、ち●ち●うっさいし！
examples.literal: Ugh, enough! Penis, penis, you're so loud!
examples.natural: Ugh, enough! Penis, penis, you're so loud!

usage_rules: Preserve repetition of embarrassing/absurd words
Don't smooth out for elegance - repetition IS the joke
Works for escalating frustration/embarrassment

patterns.japanese_structure: ～より～ (comparative with absurd element)
patterns.english_pattern: Direct comparison for absurd contrast

examples.jp: ほんとさ、うちの犬の方が利口なのおかしいでしょ
examples.literal: Honestly, it's ridiculous how much smarter my dog is than you.
examples.natural: Honestly, it's ridiculous how much smarter my dog is than you.

usage_rules: Keep absurd comparison direct
Don't soften the insult/comparison
Works for playful banter and teasing

sentence_endings.description: Japanese sentence-final particles that add nuance, confirmation-seeking, or emotional coloring - critical for natural dialogue flow

negative_vectors.description: Suppress match when particle is mid-sentence conjunction or grammatical marker, not sentence-final emotional coloring
texts: mid-sentence conjunction, connecting clause, subordinate clause marker
grammatical particle marking subject object or topic, structural case marker
quotation frame, he said that, she mentioned that, reporting clause

patterns.japanese_structure: ～だよね / ～よね
patterns.english_pattern: Right?, Don't you think?, Isn't it?

examples.jp: これ、おかしいよね？
examples.literal: This is weird, right?
examples.natural: This is weird, right?

usage_rules: Add question tags naturally: right?, yeah?, don't you think?
Can soften statements or seek agreement
Common in casual conversation for rapport building

patterns.japanese_structure: ～じゃない？ / ～じゃん
patterns.english_pattern: Isn't it?, Right?, You know

examples.jp: それ、やばいじゃない？
examples.literal: That's bad, isn't it?
examples.natural: That's bad, isn't it?

usage_rules: じゃん is more casual/assertive than じゃない
Can express obviousness or shared knowledge
Often drops question mark for statements

patterns.japanese_structure: ～かな / ～かなあ
patterns.english_pattern: I wonder, Maybe, I guess

examples.jp: 明日雨かな
examples.literal: I wonder if it will rain tomorrow
examples.natural: Wonder if it'll rain tomorrow.

usage_rules: Expresses uncertainty or tentative thinking
Can drop 'I wonder' for brevity in casual speech
Often used for self-directed questions

patterns.japanese_structure: ～んだ / ～のだ
patterns.english_pattern: You see, That's because, The thing is

examples.jp: 忙しかったんだ
examples.literal: I was busy (explanatory)
examples.natural: I was busy, you see.

usage_rules: Adds explanatory/contextual nuance to statements
Often implies 'let me explain' or 'here's why'
Can be dropped in translation if context is clear

patterns.japanese_structure: ～だろう / ～でしょう
patterns.english_pattern: Probably, I bet, Must be

examples.jp: 彼は来るだろう
examples.literal: He will probably come
examples.natural: He'll probably come.

usage_rules: Expresses probability or assumption
Can seek confirmation (with rising intonation)
だろ is casual, でしょう is polite

patterns.japanese_structure: ～のか / ～のかな
patterns.english_pattern: I wonder if, Could it be

examples.jp: 本当なのか
examples.literal: I wonder if it's true
examples.natural: Is it really true?

usage_rules: Self-directed questioning or pondering
Often internal monologue in narrative
Can drop 'I wonder' in English for directness

patterns.japanese_structure: ～っけ / ～んだっけ
patterns.english_pattern: Was it...?, Let me think, If I recall

examples.jp: 明日だっけ？
examples.literal: Was it tomorrow?
examples.natural: Was it tomorrow?

usage_rules: Indicates trying to recall information
Often translates with 'again' or 'was it'
Common in casual conversation

patterns.japanese_structure: ～わけだ / ～わけですね
patterns.english_pattern: That's why, No wonder, Naturally

examples.jp: だから遅れたわけだ
examples.literal: That's why you were late
examples.natural: So that's why you were late.

usage_rules: Expresses logical conclusion or realization
Often shows understanding of cause-effect
Can be triumphant ('I knew it!') or neutral

emotional_nuance.description: Japanese modal particles and adverbs that add speaker attitude, uncertainty, or emotional coloring - essential for authentic character voice

negative_vectors.description: Suppress match when modifier is factual/quantitative rather than emotional/attitudinal
texts: exactly three, precisely measured, specifically at noon, quantitative amount
completely, totally, absolutely, entirely certain without emotional nuance
objective narration without speaker attitude, camera-eye description, pure action

patterns.japanese_structure: なんか
patterns.english_pattern: Kind of, Somehow, Like, Sort of

examples.jp: なんか変な感じがする
examples.literal: I have a somehow strange feeling
examples.natural: Something feels off.

usage_rules: Expresses vague uncertainty or hesitation
Often untranslatable hedge - can drop entirely
Overuse creates Valley Girl speech - use sparingly

patterns.japanese_structure: ちょっと
patterns.english_pattern: A bit, Kinda, A little, (or drop entirely)

examples.jp: ちょっと難しいかも
examples.literal: It might be a bit difficult
examples.natural: Might be kinda hard.

usage_rules: Softens statements to sound less direct
Common in requests: 'ちょっと待って' = 'wait a sec'
Can often be dropped in English without losing meaning

patterns.japanese_structure: やっぱり / やはり
patterns.english_pattern: As expected, I knew it, Just as I thought, Sure enough

examples.jp: やっぱりそうだった
examples.literal: It was as expected
examples.natural: I knew it.

usage_rules: Confirms prior expectation or suspicion
やっぱり is casual, やはり is formal
Can express resignation, vindication, or confirmation

patterns.japanese_structure: さすがに
patterns.english_pattern: Even so, Even I, That's too much, As expected

examples.jp: さすがにそれは無理だ
examples.literal: Even so, that's impossible
examples.natural: Even I can't do that. / That's too much.

usage_rules: Expresses reaching a limit or exceeding expectations
Two meanings: (1) 'even I/you' (limit), (2) 'as expected'...
Context determines which meaning applies

patterns.japanese_structure: まさか
patterns.english_pattern: No way, Surely not, Don't tell me, Could it be

examples.jp: まさか、そんなことないよね？
examples.literal: Surely not, that's not the case, right?
examples.natural: No way. That can't be true, right?

usage_rules: Expresses disbelief or strong doubt
Often rhetorical: speaker knows it's true but shocked
まさかね is dismissive: 'nah, can't be'

patterns.japanese_structure: もしかして
patterns.english_pattern: Could it be, By any chance, Maybe, Perhaps

examples.jp: もしかして怒ってる？
examples.literal: By any chance, are you angry?
examples.natural: Are you mad?

usage_rules: Expresses tentative hypothesis or realization
Often marks sudden understanding or suspicion
Can be excited discovery or worried question

patterns.japanese_structure: どうせ
patterns.english_pattern: Anyway, Might as well, It's not like, In the end

examples.jp: どうせ無理だし
examples.literal: Anyway it's impossible
examples.natural: It's hopeless anyway.

usage_rules: Expresses resignation or fatalism
Often dismissive or pessimistic tone
Can justify action: 'might as well since...'

patterns.japanese_structure: 一応
patterns.english_pattern: Just in case, For now, Technically, I guess

examples.jp: 一応確認しておくね
examples.literal: I'll confirm just in case
examples.natural: I'll double-check just in case.

usage_rules: Hedges commitment or certainty
Multiple meanings: 'just in case', 'technically', 'for now'
Shows doing something without full confidence

patterns.japanese_structure: とりあえず
patterns.english_pattern: For now, First, Anyway, Let's start with

examples.jp: とりあえずビール
examples.literal: For now, beer
examples.natural: Beer to start. / I'll have a beer.

usage_rules: Taking first step without full plan
Common in restaurants: 'とりあえずビール' = 'beer to start'
Shows pragmatic approach: deal with immediate need first

action_emphasis.description: Japanese auxiliary verbs that modify action nuance - completion, trial, motion, benefit - critical for capturing character intent and emotional undertones

negative_vectors.description: Suppress match when verb is plain past tense or habitual action without auxiliary nuance
texts: simple past tense, did it, went there, ate food, basic completed action
habitual action, always does, every day, routine behavior, regular activity
stative verb, knows, understands, believes, exists, possesses

patterns.japanese_structure: ～てしまう / ～ちゃう / ～じゃう
patterns.english_pattern: Ended up, Accidentally, Completely, (or just past tense with regret)

examples.jp: 忘れてしまった
examples.literal: I completely forgot
examples.natural: I forgot. / Dammit, I forgot.

usage_rules: Expresses regret, completion, or irreversibility
ちゃう/じゃう are casual contractions of てしまう
Often implies accident or unintended consequence

patterns.japanese_structure: ～てみる
patterns.english_pattern: Try (doing), Give it a try, See what happens

examples.jp: やってみる
examples.literal: I'll try doing it
examples.natural: I'll give it a shot. / Let me try.

usage_rules: Indicates attempting action to see result
Shows experimental or tentative attitude
Often translates as 'give it a try', 'see if'

patterns.japanese_structure: ～てくる
patterns.english_pattern: (1) Come/go and do, (2) Gradually, (3) Be back (after doing)

examples.jp: 買ってくる
examples.literal: I'll go buy and come back
examples.natural: I'll go grab it. / Be right back.

usage_rules: Three meanings: (1) go-and-return action, (2) gradual cha...
Directional: action moves toward speaker
With change verbs: 'starting to', 'gradually'

patterns.japanese_structure: ～ていく
patterns.english_pattern: (1) Go and do, (2) Gradually, (3) Keep doing, (4) Will (future continuous)

examples.jp: 持っていく
examples.literal: I'll take it (and go)
examples.natural: I'll take it with me.

usage_rules: Directional: action moves away from speaker
With change verbs: ongoing change into future
Shows continuation or progression

patterns.japanese_structure: ～てくれる / ～てくれた
patterns.english_pattern: (do) for me, (emphasize gratitude or benefit received)

examples.jp: 助けてくれた
examples.literal: He helped me (I'm grateful)
examples.natural: He helped me. / Thanks for helping.

usage_rules: Shows speaker receives benefit from action
Implies gratitude or appreciation
Contrasts with てあげる (giving benefit to others)

patterns.japanese_structure: ～ておく
patterns.english_pattern: Do (in advance), Leave (in a state), Prepare, Set up

examples.jp: 買っておく
examples.literal: I'll buy it in advance
examples.natural: I'll buy it ahead of time. / I'll grab it now.

usage_rules: Indicates preparatory action for future benefit
Can mean 'leave in a state' or 'do in advance'
とく is casual contraction

patterns.japanese_structure: ～てしまった (past form emphasis)
patterns.english_pattern: Ended up (doing), It's too late, Already (regret emphasis)

examples.jp: 言ってしまった
examples.literal: I ended up saying it
examples.natural: I said it. / Crap, I said it.

usage_rules: Past tense of てしまう - emphasizes completion
Often expresses regret or 'too late now'
Can show surprise at own actions

onomatopoeia.description: Japanese onomatopoeia and mimetic words (擬音語・擬態語) requiring creative English equivalents — 10,512 corpus hits across 148 EPUBs, critical for light novel atmosphere

negative_vectors.description: Suppress match on literal mechanical sounds, environmental noise descriptions, or non-mimetic katakana words
texts: literal machine sound, engine noise, alarm ringing, phone buzzing, mechanical beeping
foreign loanword in katakana, コンピュータ computer, テレビ television, borrowed English word
name in katakana, character name, place name, brand name, proper noun

patterns.japanese_structure: ドキドキ / ドキッ (heartbeat/excitement/nervousness)
patterns.english_pattern: Context-dependent: heart pounding/flutter/skip a beat/pulse racing

examples.jp: 彼女の顔を見た瞬間、ドキドキが止まらなかった。
examples.literal: The moment I saw her face, the doki-doki wouldn't stop.
examples.natural: The moment I saw her face, my heart wouldn't stop racing.

usage_rules: Romantic: 'heart skipped a beat', 'pulse quickened', 'flu...
Nervous: 'heart hammering', 'pounding', 'racing'
Sudden (ドキッ): 'jolt', 'startled', 'caught off guard'

patterns.japanese_structure: ニコニコ / ニコッ (smiling/beaming)
patterns.english_pattern: beaming/grinning/with a smile/all smiles

examples.jp: 彼女はニコニコしながら手を振った。
examples.literal: She waved while niko-niko-ing.
examples.natural: She waved, all smiles.

usage_rules: Continuous (ニコニコ): 'beaming', 'all smiles', 'with a warm ...
Sudden (ニコッ): 'flashed a smile', 'grinned', 'broke into a...
AVOID: mechanical 'smiled' for every ニコ — vary descriptions

patterns.japanese_structure: ニヤリ / ニヤニヤ / ニヤッ (smirk/mischievous grin)
patterns.english_pattern: smirked/grinned/sly smile/that look

examples.jp: 彰はニヤリと笑った。
examples.literal: Akira niyari-laughed.
examples.natural: Akira flashed a smug grin.

usage_rules: ニヤリ (brief smirk): 'smirked', 'flashed a grin', 'sly smile'
ニヤニヤ (continuous): 'kept grinning', 'that smug look', 'co...
Playful: 'mischievous grin' — Malicious: 'sinister smile'

patterns.japanese_structure: チラッ / チラチラ / ちらり (quick glance)
patterns.english_pattern: glanced/stole a look/peeked/snuck a glance

examples.jp: 彼女の方をチラッと見た。
examples.literal: I chira-looked toward her.
examples.natural: I stole a quick glance her way.

usage_rules: Single (チラッ/チラリ): 'glanced', 'stole a look', 'peeked'
Repeated (チラチラ): 'kept glancing', 'sneaking looks', 'eyes...
Furtive: 'snuck a glance', 'peeked' — Casual: 'glanced over'

patterns.japanese_structure: ぼーっと / ボーッと (spacing out/dazed)
patterns.english_pattern: spacing out/zoning out/staring blankly/in a daze

examples.jp: 窓の外をぼーっと眺めていた。
examples.literal: I was bo~tto gazing outside the window.
examples.natural: I was staring blankly out the window.

usage_rules: Inattentive: 'spacing out', 'zoning out', 'lost in thought'
Stunned: 'in a daze', 'frozen', 'mesmerized'
Idle: 'staring off into space', 'mind wandering'

patterns.japanese_structure: ソワソワ / モジモジ (fidgeting/restless/shy)
patterns.english_pattern: fidgeting/restless/squirming/shifting nervously

examples.jp: 彼女はモジモジしながら言った。
examples.literal: She said while moji-moji-ing.
examples.natural: She fidgeted shyly as she spoke.

usage_rules: ソワソワ (anxious/restless): 'fidgeting', 'restless', 'on edg...
モジモジ (shy/embarrassed): 'squirming', 'fidgeting shyly', '...
Action description: describe the fidgeting behavior inste...

patterns.japanese_structure: グッと / ぐっと (tightly/firmly/with resolve)
patterns.english_pattern: clenched/gripped/tightened/steeled oneself

examples.jp: 拳をグッと握りしめた。
examples.literal: I gu-tto clenched my fist.
examples.natural: I balled my fists tight.

usage_rules: Physical (gripping): 'clenched', 'tightened', 'squeezed',...
Emotional (enduring): 'bit back', 'swallowed', 'held in',...
ぎゅっと (squeeze): 'hugged tight', 'latched on', 'pressed cl...

patterns.japanese_structure: キャッ / キャー / きゃあ (shriek/squeal)
patterns.english_pattern: shrieked/squealed/yelped/screamed

examples.jp: 「キャッ！」と小さな悲鳴を上げた。
examples.literal: 'Kya!' she raised a small scream.
examples.natural: She let out a small yelp.

usage_rules: Surprise: 'yelp', 'shriek', 'Eek!'
Fangirling (キャーキャー): 'squealing', 'shrieking', 'going wild'
Embarrassment: 'Eek!', 'Aah!', or describe the reaction i...

giving_receiving.description: Japanese giving/receiving auxiliary verbs (やりもらい) that encode social dynamics and favors — 21,405 corpus hits, critical for natural English dialogue

negative_vectors.description: Suppress match on plain transitive actions without social/benefactive nuance
texts: self-directed action, I did it myself, acting alone, independent action without beneficiary
物を渡す physically handing object, literal give and take, commercial transaction, buying selling
causative without social nuance, mechanical cause and effect, physics, natural process

patterns.japanese_structure: てくれる / てくれた / てくれない (doing something for the speaker's benefit)
patterns.english_pattern: Restructure to show appreciation/benefit naturally

examples.jp: シャーロットさんは日本への想いを教えてくれた。

rag_retrieval_strategy.description: How to retrieve patterns during translation

steps.name: Parse Japanese structure
steps.method: Identify particles, connectors, sentence patterns in source via grammar_pattern_detector (16 regex categories, 97 patterns)

steps.name: Batch embed & vector search
steps.method: Batch-embed all detected pattern contexts in 1 API call → ChromaDB cosine search filtered by category → return top-k matches

steps.name: Negative anchor filtering
steps.method: For each match, compute cosine similarity between query and category negative_vectors. If similarity > 0.72, apply proportional penalty (up to -0.15) to suppress false positives. Example: intensifiers pattern won't fire on 'completely certain' sentences.

steps.name: Threshold gate
steps.method: After penalty adjustment: similarity ≥ 0.78 → inject into prompt as few-shot example. 0.65-0.78 → log for audit. < 0.65 → discard.

steps.name: Priority ranking & injection
steps.method: Sort by CATEGORY_PRIORITIES (10=highest), then similarity. Inject top 3-5 high-confidence patterns as context for LLM.

steps.name: Post-process validation
steps.method: Check if LLM applied pattern correctly, suggest fixes if needed

negative_anchor_system.description: Prevents false-positive pattern matches by penalizing queries that are semantically close to known anti-patterns for each category

implementation.storage: negative_vectors.texts[] in each category — 3 English sentences describing what this category is NOT
implementation.embedding: Lazily batch-embedded on first get_bulk_guidance() call via Gemini Embedding 001, cached as numpy arrays
implementation.scoring: cosine_sim(query_embedding, negative_vector) → if > NEGATIVE_ANCHOR_THRESHOLD (0.72), penalty = overshoot × NEGATIVE_ANCHOR_PENALTY (0.15)
implementation.effect: A match with raw_similarity=0.82 but neg_penalty=0.08 becomes effective_similarity=0.74, dropping it from inject tier to log-only tier

covered_categories: response_particles — negatives: narration, formal prose, silent thought
sentence_endings — negatives: mid-sentence particles, case markers
emotional_nuance — negatives: quantitative, absolute certainty, objective
action_emphasis — negatives: simple past, habitual, stative
onomatopoeia — negatives: mechanical sounds, loanwords, proper nouns
giving_receiving — negatives: self-action, transactions, mechanical cause
inner_monologue — negatives: direct dialogue, objective narration, intentional
desire_intention — negatives: completed fact, obligation, capability
structure_particles — negatives: basic case particles, simple copula

pov_mechanics.description: JP→EN structural patterns for POV identity, physiological emotion, camera sequencing, and world vocabulary neutrality. Cross-reference: english_grammar_validation_t1.json v1.5 pov_mechanics_violations (negative detection) + literacy_techniques.json v2.0 narrative_techniques subtechniques (technique definitions). Source: Masquerade Confidence Ch.1 [MF Bunko J] review (2026-02-21).

patterns.japanese_structure: 「彼」/「彼女」 used as scare-quoted pronoun for a performed identity (not the narrator's true self)
patterns.english_pattern: Preserve scare quotes verbatim: '"his" face' / '"her" voice' — do NOT normalize to plain possessive pronoun

examples.jp: 「彼」の顔が剥がれていく
examples.literal: his face peeled away
examples.natural: "his" face peeled away
examples.context: Con-artist discarding a performed persona — scare quotes signal 'Arthur' was always a costume, not a person

usage_rules: Check if preceding paragraphs established the character as performing an alias/role
If yes, mirror JP scare-quotes exactly in EN
The POV shift from '"he"' to 'I' is the scene's entire dramatic payload — never smooth it

patterns.japanese_structure: 肺腑から / 胸の裡 / 胃の底 + emotional purgation verb (排出する、吐き出す、流し出す)
patterns.english_pattern: Translate via body-organ precision + physical sensation noun — NEVER name the emotion directly

examples.jp: 胸の裡でくすぶる妻への愛情を、全て排出するかのように
examples.literal: as if expelling all the love smoldering in his chest
examples.natural: draining out whatever warmth had been kept there / pulling the breath all the way from his gut
examples.context: Con-artist exiting the husband persona — must feel clinical/cold, not melodramatic

examples.jp: 肺腑から息という息を静かに吐き出していく
examples.literal: quietly exhaling every breath from his lungs
examples.natural: He exhaled slowly, pulling the breath all the way from his gut
examples.context: Physiological action precedes the purgation — must be rendered as body mechanics, not emotion report

usage_rules: BANNED: 'purging his love', 'expelling his affection', 'releasing her grief'
PREFERRED: 'draining whatever warmth...', 'emptying his chest of its last heat', 'pulling it out from his gut'
When emotion must be implied, use physical sensation nouns: heat, warmth, weight, pressure — not love, grief, guilt

patterns.japanese_structure: Sentence 1: Subject A + perception verb + object/scene. Sentence 2: そんなAを / Aの横顔を + Subject B + observation verb
japanese_indicators: そんな彼女を眺めながら
うっとりと眺めていた / 見つめていた (A's gaze)
満足げに眺めつつ (B watching A)
patterns.english_pattern: Preserve as two separate sentences. Shot 1: A's gaze + object. Shot 2: B observing A. NEVER collapse into 'B watched A [verb] the [object]'

examples.jp: 新妻の瞳がうっとりと眺めていた。そんな妻の横顔を満足げに眺めつつ、「彼」は…
examples.literal: He watched his wife gaze dreamily at the dinner table.
examples.natural: She gazed dreamily at the white-linen dinner table — the silver candelabras were a perfect touch. He watched his wife's profile from across the room, deeply satisfied.
examples.context: Con-artist establishing surveillance gaze over new wife — the double-shot carries the predator-prey subtext

usage_rules: BANNED COLLAPSE: 'B watched A gaze at X' — merges two shots into one flat statement
Shot 1 must end before B appears. B's sentence begins with the object of Shot 1 ('his wife's profile') not a merge verb
Trigger: JP sentence 2 starts with そんな[A]を or [A]の[body part]を

patterns.japanese_structure: 軍役 / 内戦 / 戦友 / 名家 / 使用人 used in non-realistic alt-history/fantasy world
patterns.english_pattern: Use period-neutral equivalents — avoid real-world-era-anchoring vocabulary (no 'trenches', 'samurai', 'Victorian', 'feudal serf')

examples.jp: 数年間の泥臭い軍役
examples.literal: years in the trenches
examples.natural: years of brutal military service / years in the field / years on campaign
examples.source: Masquerade Confidence Ch.1 [MF Bunko J] — set in fictional Republic of Colonials, not WWI
examples.context: Novel's world is a fictional post-revolution republic — 'trenches' anchors to WWI and breaks immersion

examples.jp: 戦友との絆
examples.literal: camaraderie in the trenches / trench mates
examples.natural: the bonds forged with fellow soldiers / camaraderie built under fire

examples.jp: 使用人
examples.literal: servants
examples.natural: household staff / retainers / staff
examples.context: In aristocratic fantasy, 'servants' is acceptable; 'serfs' or 'Victorian footmen' are not unless setting demands it

usage_rules: Check manifest.json genre/setting tags before choosing military vocabulary
If setting = steampunk / alternate-history / fantasy → use neutral military/social vocabulary
If setting = historical fiction → period-specific terms are correct
BANNED in non-historical settings: trenches, the front (WWII), samurai, daimyo, Victorian, Edwardian, the Great War

phase_2_translation.action: Analyze source text, retrieve 3-5 most relevant patterns, inject as examples

phase_3_critics.action: Check if opportunities for natural idioms were missed, flag for revision
